Results show large performance drops versus standard VQA and that Q-Only is substantially worse, confirming visual grounding. Ablations reveal limited gains from stronger visual backbones (ResNet18→50→152) compared to the benefit of adding retrieved knowledge, and oracles indicate that retrieved knowledge provides complementary signals to VQA models.
paper_title: OK-VQA: A Visual Question Answering Benchmark Requiring External Knowledge

problem: VLN agents struggle to generalize instruction understanding from seen to unseen environments because training instructions have limited n-gram overlap with evaluation and encoders trained from scratch or using static embeddings overfit to seen data.
method: Replace static word embeddings with contextualized representations from large-scale pretrained language models (BERT, GPT) inside the instruction encoder, and fine-tune them for VLN. The encoder attends to the instruction conditioned on the agent’s state, and training follows a two-stage scheme: (i) freeze the pretrained LM to supply embeddings while training the rest, then (ii) fine-tune all parameters with a smaller learning rate. This LM adaptation markedly improves robustness on unseen environments (e.g., BERT > GPT > LSTM; smaller seen–unseen gap) and boosts SPL on R2R.
paper_title: Robust Navigation with Language Pretraining and Stochastic Sampling

problem: Sequential action decoding suffers from exposure bias: teacher-forcing mismatches test-time behavior, while student-forcing can cause inefficient exploration and derailment, especially with short panoramic trajectories.
method: Introduce stochastic action sampling that, at each decoding step, chooses between the teacher action and a sampled action from the current policy with a fixed probability ε (unlike standard scheduled sampling’s decaying schedule). This blends the stability of teacher-forcing with the realism of learning from one’s own actions, enabling recovery from mistakes during long rollouts. The scheme outperforms pure teacher- or student-forcing on unseen environments and, when combined with LM pretraining, yields state-of-the-art R2R performance (e.g., 53% SPL on test unseen).
paper_title: Robust Navigation with Language Pretraining and Stochastic Sampling

problem: Multimodal and language models typically rely on task-specific heads/adapters and heterogeneous task formulations, and often entangle visual representations with downstream detectors, hindering zero-shot transfer and true unification across tasks and modalities.
method: OFA casts all uni- and cross-modal tasks as instruction-driven sequence-to-sequence generation with a single encoder–decoder Transformer and no task- or modality-specific layers. It builds a shared multimodal token vocabulary by discretizing images into short code sequences and representing objects as label tokens plus discretized bounding-box coordinates, while encoding images via ResNet patch features. Handcrafted instructions unify task interfaces across pretraining, finetuning, and inference, enabling a task- and modality-agnostic I/O and architecture.
paper_title: OFA: Unifying Architectures, Tasks, and Modalities through a Simple Sequence-to-Sequence Learning Framework

problem: Generative Seq2Seq models are inefficient for classification and can produce invalid labels outside a closed candidate set.
method: OFA introduces Trie-based constrained decoding/training: a prefix tree over candidate label tokens prunes invalid continuations by masking their logits during both training and inference. This constrains beam search to valid labels and improves efficiency and accuracy on classification-formulated tasks such as VQA, visual entailment, and ImageNet classification.
paper_title: OFA: Unifying Architectures, Tasks, and Modalities through a Simple Sequence-to-Sequence Learning Framework

problem: Pretraining often lacks task comprehensiveness and fine-grained cross-modal alignment, limiting performance and transfer on diverse multimodal tasks, including localization and text-to-image generation.
method: OFA jointly pretrains on a broad set of tasks within the same instruction-Seq2Seq schema: cross-modal (visual grounding, grounded captioning, image–text matching, image captioning, VQA) and uni-modal (image infilling, object detection, text infilling). Bounding boxes are generated as sequences of four location tokens, and text-to-image generation is realized by predicting discrete image codes and decoding them with a VQGAN codebook at inference. Ablations show region-prediction tasks (detection, grounding) notably boost captioning and VQA, demonstrating the benefit of comprehensive, alignment-focused multitask pretraining.
paper_title: OFA: Unifying Architectures, Tasks, and Modalities through a Simple Sequence-to-Sequence Learning Framework

problem: Prompt learning for CLIP typically tunes only the text branch, leaving the image encoder unadapted. This partial (uni-modal) adaptation limits alignment between vision and language spaces and hurts few-shot generalization to novel classes and datasets.
method: MaPLe performs multi-modal prompt learning by inserting learnable context tokens into both CLIP’s language and vision transformers. It uses hierarchical (deep) prompting, adding prompts to the first J transformer blocks in each branch to progressively adapt stage-wise features, while keeping all original CLIP weights frozen. This joint, branch-aware prompting adapts both representation spaces for improved V-L alignment and generalization.
paper_title: MaPLe: Multi-modal Prompt Learning

problem: Naively learning prompts in both branches independently offers no cross-modal interaction, resulting in weak synergy between vision and language prompts and suboptimal alignment.
method: MaPLe introduces a vision–language coupling function F that explicitly conditions the vision prompts on the language prompts per layer, via a linear projection ˜P_k = F_k(P_k) from text to vision dimensions. This bridge enables mutual gradient flow and shared embedding space across modalities, promoting synergy. Compared to independent V–L prompting, this coupling yields better generalization (e.g., higher HM on 11 datasets).
paper_title: MaPLe: Multi-modal Prompt Learning

problem: Shallow prompting at only the input layer cannot capture the stage-wise contextual relationships needed to effectively adapt frozen transformer features, limiting robustness under distribution shifts.
method: MaPLe learns separate prompts across multiple early transformer blocks (depth J) in both branches (deep prompting), and in the vision branch shares prompts across stages to exploit correlated features. Only the prompts (and the V–L coupling) are trained. Ablations show performance improves with depth and peaks at J=9, boosting base-to-novel generalization and robustness under cross-dataset and domain shifts.
paper_title: MaPLe: Multi-modal Prompt Learning

problem: VLN agents trained on shorter instructions struggle to generalize to longer, multi-step instructions, leading to poor instruction-following and long-horizon reasoning.
method: Introduce BabyWalk, which decomposes a long instruction into micro-instructions (BABY-STEPs) and executes them sequentially while conditioning on a learned context. A dedicated memory buffer summarizes past micro-instructions and their executed sub-trajectories via a hierarchical, ‘forgetting’-weighted summary that emphasizes recent steps (γ≈0.5) over distant history. The policy is an LSTM with cross-modal attention that conditions on the current micro-instruction and the summarized context. Ablations show this memory design outperforms no memory and LSTM-based summaries and significantly boosts CLS/SDTW generalization to longer tasks.
paper_title: BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps

problem: Direct reinforcement learning on long-horizon VLN instructions is unstable, and imitation learning on whole trajectories does not adequately prepare agents for long, uncertain executions.
method: Use a two-phase training pipeline: (1) micro-level imitation learning where each BABY-STEP is learned independently with the agent 'preset' in expert context to match expert sub-trajectories; (2) curriculum-based reinforcement learning that progressively increases the number of consecutive BABY-STEPs the agent must execute (lectures), providing expert history up to the start and optimizing a fidelity-oriented reward (CLS) via policy gradients. This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.
paper_title: BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps

problem: VLN lacks benchmarks and aligned supervision to evaluate and train agents on substantially longer instruction-following tasks.
method: Create ROOM6ROOM and ROOM8ROOM by concatenating ROOM2ROOM paths to extend task length to 6- and 8-room trajectories. Provide automatic supervision by segmenting instructions into BABY-STEPs using POS- and landmark-based templates, then aligning these to sub-trajectories via a weakly supervised multi-label visual landmark classifier and a dynamic programming alignment procedure. Release the datasets and BABY-STEP segmentations/alignments to support training and evaluation on long-instruction VLN.
paper_title: BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps

problem: Pretrained vision-language models like CLIP cannot natively handle user-specific (personalized) visual concepts from only a few positive images, while preserving open-vocabulary reasoning and without task-specific fine-tuning or negative examples.
method: PALAVRA personalizes a frozen V&L model by expanding its input vocabulary with a new token embedding per personalized concept, which can be used in free-form text. It first trains an inversion mapping f_θ (a Deep Sets encoder) from sets of CLIP image embeddings to a token embedding in CLIP’s word-embedding space using a contrastive cycle-consistency loss that aligns template-sentence text embeddings with averaged image embeddings (Sec. 4.1, Fig. 3). Given a user’s few images, f_θ initializes the concept token w_c, which is then fine-tuned by optimizing similarity to the images while contrasting against the concept type embedding (a ‘super-concept’)—enabling learning from positive-only examples (Sec. 4.2). The CLIP encoders remain frozen, so prior capabilities are preserved and the learned token can be inserted into arbitrary sentences at inference (Secs. 4, 4.3; Fig. 2).
paper_title: "This is my unicorn, Fluffy": Personalizing frozen vision-language representations

problem: Learning f_θ from limited visual concepts (e.g., COCO) does not generalize well to out-of-vocabulary concepts, and CLIP’s text and image embedding distributions are misaligned, impeding training with textual data.
method: They synthesize diverse textual training data by replacing concept types in COCO captions with semantically similar types drawn from a large 20K-type vocabulary, and train f_θ alternately on images and these augmented captions (Sec. 4.1). To bridge the modality gap, an alignment matrix A is learned to map CLIP text embeddings toward the image-embedding region before feeding them to f_θ; A is trained jointly and used during personalization (Secs. 4.1, A.3–A.4). Ablations show text augmentation and A substantially improve retrieval MRR (e.g., +~16% on DeepFashion2 and +~6% on YTVOS for augmentation; +~8% and +~5% for A) (Table 2, Sec. 7).
paper_title: "This is my unicorn, Fluffy": Personalizing frozen vision-language representations

problem: There is no benchmark to evaluate few-shot personalization of V&L models across tasks and rich referring language, with realistic distractors and multiple instances.
method: They introduce two PerVL benchmarks: (1) a DeepFashion2-based personalized fashion retrieval set with short and detailed AMT captions, across 100 unique items, and (2) a Youtube-VOS-based dataset for both personalized retrieval and referring-expression segmentation, with AMT captions and multiple similar instances per scene (Secs. 5.1–5.2). Using these, PALAVRA achieves state-of-the-art results, including a 51.1% relative MRR gain over the strongest retrieval baseline on DeepFashion2 Rich queries (28.4 vs 18.8) and dominant IoU-threshold curves for segmentation on YTVOS (Table 1, Fig. 6–7, Secs. 6.1–6.2).
paper_title: "This is my unicorn, Fluffy": Personalizing frozen vision-language representations

problem: Pre-trained general-domain vision-and-language (V&L) models underperform on chest X-rays due to a large domain gap and scarce high-quality labels; object-centric detectors (e.g., Faster R-CNN/BUTD) miss non-object-like medical abnormalities.
method: Proposes BERTHop, a single-stream transformer V&L model that replaces object detectors with an unsupervised, data-efficient visual encoder (PixelHop++). PixelHop++ extracts multi-level, multi-frequency image features that are PCA-reduced and concatenated into Q visual tokens (≈15) of dimension 2048 before fusion with text. The transformer is initialized with BlueBERT (pre-trained on biomedical/clinical text), avoiding expensive cross-modal pretraining. On OpenI, BERTHop achieves 0.9823 AUC, outperforming TieNet by 1.73 points while training on only 2,912 pairs versus 108,948 for TieNet.
paper_title: BERTHop: An Effective Vision-and-Language Model for Chest X-ray Disease Diagnosis

problem: Determining an effective initialization for medical V&L transformers where general-domain V&L pretraining transfers poorly.
method: Initialize the transformer with in-domain text-only pretraining (BlueBERT) instead of general V&L pretraining. When paired with PixelHop++, BlueBERT boosts average AUC by 6.46% over BERT; with BUTD visual features, performance remains low and is insensitive to initialization (≈83.09–85.64% AUC). This demonstrates that in-domain single-modality (text) pretraining can be more beneficial than general-domain cross-modal pretraining for medical V&L.
paper_title: BERTHop: An Effective Vision-and-Language Model for Chest X-ray Disease Diagnosis

problem: Identifying a visual encoder better suited to medical images for V&L fusion and abnormality localization.
method: Systematically compare BUTD, ChexNet, and PixelHop++ within the same BlueBERT-initialized transformer. PixelHop++ delivers the best average AUC (0.9823) versus ChexNet (0.8798) and BUTD (0.8564), and its output channels highlight radiologist-annotated pathology regions. The unsupervised, multi-frequency features from PixelHop++ more effectively capture medical abnormalities than object-centric detectors or CNN features pre-trained on ChestX-ray14.
paper_title: BERTHop: An Effective Vision-and-Language Model for Chest X-ray Disease Diagnosis

problem: Collecting high-quality, diverse trajectories for training GUI agents is costly and brittle: task-driven pipelines depend on human-written or pre-defined tasks, limiting coverage and often yielding incoherent or non-executable trajectories in dynamic environments.
method: OS-Genesis reverses the conventional process with an interaction-driven pipeline. It first performs human-free, rule-based exploration in Android emulators and a Chrome browser by traversing UI elements with actions A={CLICK, TYPE, SCROLL}, invoking GPT-4o only to generate contents for input fields. This yields massive pre/post state-action triplets (pre/post screenshots and the executed action). An annotation model then conducts reverse task synthesis: each triplet is converted into an atomic low-level instruction and further contextualized into a high-level, goal-oriented task grounded in the observed UI changes. These synthesized high-level tasks are executed to produce complete trajectories used for SFT. This bottom-up, interaction-first design bridges abstract instructions with dynamic GUIs and increases task and trajectory diversity without human supervision.
paper_title: OS-Genesis: Automating GUI Agent Trajectory Construction via Reverse Task Synthesis

problem: Automatically generated trajectories often contain partial progress or errors; binary filtering discards valuable exploration signals and harms data efficiency, while unfiltered training mixes low- and high-quality data indiscriminately.
method: A Trajectory Reward Model (TRM) built on GPT-4o assigns a graded reward R∈[1,5] to each trajectory based on completion and coherence. Given a trajectory’s low-level instruction history and the last three screenshots, TRM scores the rollout; training then samples trajectories proportionally to their rewards (Algorithm 1). This graded, trajectory-specific evaluation preserves informative partial runs while prioritizing higher-quality data, outperforming both no-reward and binary labeler filtering strategies.
paper_title: OS-Genesis: Automating GUI Agent Trajectory Construction via Reverse Task Synthesis

problem: LVLMs integrate visual information into semantic representations across layers, but their attention underweights these vision-enriched semantic tokens, leading to hallucinations due to a misalignment between attention distribution and actual information flow.
method: SEVI (training-free) identifies attention heads that already emphasize semantic representations and aligns all heads to their patterns via a two-stage optimization with smoothing. First, it selects heads allocating >50% of attention to semantic tokens and splits them into Global Semantic Heads and Core Semantic Heads using a peak-threshold κ on max-vs-sum semantic attention. Stage 1: average-pool Global Semantic Heads to guide all non-semantic heads; Stage 2: max-pool Core Semantic Heads to guide Global Semantic Heads. A weighted smoothing factor ω blends target and original weights to preserve stability. Applied mainly to higher layers, this alignment significantly reduces hallucinations on CHAIR, DetailCaps, and AMBER across multiple LVLMs.
paper_title: Mitigating Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models through Aligning Attention Distribution to Information Flow

problem: Increasing attention to semantic representations can inadvertently amplify language priors, which also cause hallucinations.
method: Integrate CICD to suppress detrimental language priors while preserving visual signal. CICD constructs a negative context using a different image and performs contrastive decoding, with the subtraction strength α modulated by the Jensen–Shannon divergence between logits of regular and cross-image inputs. Ablations show that removing CICD degrades performance, confirming it mitigates priors exacerbated by semantic-focused attention.
paper_title: Mitigating Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models through Aligning Attention Distribution to Information Flow

problem: Real-world use requires balancing hallucination suppression against descriptive richness; a one-size-fits-all setting is insufficient.
method: Expose simple controls over conservativeness via start layer (SL) and smoothing weight ω to trade off detail vs. caution. A Focused mode (e.g., SL=5, ω=4) aggressively reduces hallucinations at the cost of recall, while a Balanced mode (e.g., SL=9, ω=0.5) preserves more detail with low hallucination rates. Experiments also support a layer-wise focus principle—emphasize visual tokens in early layers and semantic tokens in later layers—showing added gains when combining lower-layer visual attention boosting (e.g., PAI on layers 2–15) with SEVI in higher layers (16–31).
paper_title: Mitigating Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models through Aligning Attention Distribution to Information Flow

problem: The severity and characteristics of object hallucination in large vision-language models (LVLMs) are unclear, and existing caption-based metrics like CHAIR may be unreliable for LVLM evaluation.
method: The paper conducts a systematic empirical study on five LVLMs (e.g., LLaVA, MiniGPT-4, InstructBLIP) using MSCOCO and the CHAIR I/S metrics under two captioning prompts. It finds LVLMs often hallucinate more than smaller VLPMs (e.g., LLaVA CHAIR S 32.7 vs OSCAR base 13.0), and that CHAIR is sensitive to instruction phrasing and caption length, leading to instability and parsing challenges for LVLM-style generations. This motivates the need for a more stable and fair evaluation approach tailored to LVLMs.
paper_title: Evaluating Object Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models

problem: The underlying drivers of LVLM object hallucination—particularly the role of instruction data distributions—are not well understood.
method: The authors hypothesize that LVLMs tend to hallucinate (1) objects that frequently appear in instruction data and (2) objects that frequently co-occur with ground-truth objects. They verify this via qualitative analyses (hallucination counts vs. object frequency/co-occurrence) and introduce top-k hit ratios HR_A and HR_C to quantify correlations, showing substantial overlap (e.g., HR_A@10≈0.46–0.55; HR_C@10≈0.56–0.66, increasing with k). These results indicate LVLMs disproportionately hallucinate frequent and co-occurring objects, informing targeted evaluation sampling strategies.
paper_title: Evaluating Object Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models

problem: Evaluating LVLM object hallucination needs to be stable across prompts, fair, and scalable to datasets without human annotations, while avoiding brittle caption parsing.
method: They propose POPE (Polling-based Object Probing Evaluation), which reframes hallucination assessment as binary Yes/No questions about object presence (e.g., “Is there a <object> in the image?”) with a 1:1 balance of ground-truth and nonexistent objects. POPE introduces three negative sampling regimes—Random, Popular (top frequent objects), and Adversarial (top co-occurring with present objects)—to control difficulty. It scales to unannotated datasets by extracting objects via automatic segmentation (SEEM). Experiments show POPE yields consistent model rankings, reveals strong Yes-bias/overconfidence in several LVLMs (~95–100% Yes), has low variance across prompt phrasings, and maintains trends when applied to A-OKVQA/GQA using SEEM.
paper_title: Evaluating Object Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models

problem: Open-vocabulary detection needs to learn from massive web image–text pairs, but captions lack instance-level boxes, VL distillation suffers domain gaps, and pseudo-labeling limits accuracy and scalability.
method: DetCLIPv2 performs end-to-end joint pre-training on detection, grounding, and raw image–text pairs under a unified formulation, learning fine-grained word–region alignment directly from captions. For each noun phrase, it matches the best region proposal via a max-similarity (1-to-1) set alignment and optimizes a text-to-image contrastive loss only, which mitigates the partial-label problem in web data. It selects proposals for contrast by scoring regions with the maximum similarity to all concepts in the local batch, focusing on text-relevant regions. Alternating data types during training creates a flywheel between localization and recognition, and using low-resolution inputs for web pairs enables efficient scaling (e.g., 13× more pairs with similar training time).
paper_title: DetCLIPv2: Scalable Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Pre-training via Word-Region Alignment

problem: During weakly supervised phases with grounding or image–text pairs, the detector’s regression branch lacks direct supervision and does not benefit from VL objectives, hurting localization.
method: DetCLIPv2 inserts a lightweight deformable convolution at the start of the classification head that uses features from the regression head to compute offsets and modulation, allowing gradients from classification to flow into regression. This improves spatial aggregation and localization ability when training without box supervision, delivering clear AP gains with negligible training-time overhead.
paper_title: DetCLIPv2: Scalable Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Pre-training via Word-Region Alignment

problem: Contrastive metrics for caption evaluation (e.g., CLIP-Score) are trained on noisy web alt-text and exhibit style and distribution mismatch with curated captioning benchmarks; learning directly on small clean datasets underutilizes contrastive learning, leading to weaker alignment with human judgments and limited hallucination sensitivity.
method: PAC-S finetunes a CLIP dual-encoder by positive-augmented contrastive learning on a curated dataset (COCO), while augmenting each real image–caption pair (v, t) with synthetic positives in both modalities: a caption t′ generated from v (BLIP) and an image v′ generated from t (Stable Diffusion). Training uses a symmetric InfoNCE loss over real and generated cross-modal matches, combining losses for (V,T), (V′,T), and (V,T′) with temperature and weights (λv, λt), and only optimizes the final projection heads to the shared embedding. The resulting embedding provides a reference-free score via scaled cosine similarity, and a reference-based variant via the harmonic mean of the reference-free score and the maximum similarity to reference captions.
paper_title: Positive-Augmented Contrastive Learning for Image and Video Captioning Evaluation

problem: Evaluating video captions requires assessing both global video–caption alignment and fine-grained grounding between words and frames; existing approaches (e.g., EMScore) rely on CLIP embeddings and can be improved by better-aligned multimodal features.
method: PAC-S is extended to videos by using the positive-augmented CLIP encoders to compute (i) a coarse-grained similarity between the average-pooled frame embeddings and the whole-sentence embedding, and (ii) a fine-grained score from pairwise word–frame similarities aggregated with a TF-IDF–weighted F1. These components are combined into the final score, with a reference-based variant formed via a harmonic mean with the best reference-caption match.
paper_title: Positive-Augmented Contrastive Learning for Image and Video Captioning Evaluation

problem: Most image fusion methods over-rely on pixel-level visual cues and superficial visual semantics, neglecting deeper text-level semantics that could better guide what content to preserve from each source image.
method: FILM introduces explicit language-model guidance into image fusion. It builds paired, image-specific textual descriptions by prompting BLIP2 (image caption), GRIT (dense caption), and SAM (segment masks) into ChatGPT to generate detailed paragraphs for each source image; these are encoded with a frozen BLIP-2 text encoder and fused via concatenation. The fused text features serve as Queries in cross-attention layers, while visual features from Restormer+CNN encoders act as Keys/Values, thereby guiding which visual details to extract and merge from each source. A Restormer+CNN decoder then reconstructs the fused image. This is claimed as the first instance of using explicit LLM-derived textual guidance for image fusion and uses a text-to-vision cross-attention design (Q=text, K/V=vision).
paper_title: Image Fusion via Vision-Language Model

problem: There is no standardized vision-language resource for image fusion, making it difficult to study and reproduce text-guided fusion approaches across tasks and datasets.
method: The paper releases the Vision-Language Fusion (VLF) dataset: ChatGPT-generated paragraph descriptions for all image pairs in eight widely used fusion datasets across four tasks (infrared-visible, medical, multi-exposure, multi-focus). Prompts are constructed from BLIP2 captions, GRIT dense captions, and SAM masks to drive ChatGPT, yielding 7,040 paragraphs averaging seven sentences and 186 words per description. This resource is intended to facilitate future research on VLM-based image fusion.
paper_title: Image Fusion via Vision-Language Model

problem: Conventional GPR surveys collect scans along pre-defined straight grid lines, yielding pseudo-3D reconstructions and limiting accuracy when the sensor moves arbitrarily; accurate 3D migration requires precise sensor pose, including rotations, during data acquisition.
method: Introduce a random-motion-aware 3D GPR migration pipeline that fuses visual-inertial odometry (VIF) to estimate the 6-DoF pose of the GPR device and synchronizes it with A/B-scan data. The migration performs back-projection while updating the antenna pose with measured rotations (Eq. 5), enabling data collection in any direction and constructing real 3D GPR images rather than pseudo-3D. This pose- and rotation-aware migration improves target reconstruction compared with traditional grid-based approaches.
paper_title: GPR-based Subsurface Object Detection and Reconstruction Using Random Motion and DepthNet

problem: Background clutter and noise in GPR B-scan images degrade migration quality by introducing spurious reflections that obscure subsurface targets.
method: Apply Faster R-CNN to detect B-scan hyperbolic features and define bounding-box regions of interest (RoIs). Only B-scan data within detected RoIs are passed to the migration, while data outside are discarded as noise, effectively performing data-driven denoising prior to reconstruction. This targeted RoI filtering improves the clarity of migrated 3D images and provides an explicit noise map from removed regions.
paper_title: GPR-based Subsurface Object Detection and Reconstruction Using Random Motion and DepthNet

problem: Accurate migration depends on subsurface dielectric (permittivity) to convert travel time to depth, but dielectric is typically unknown and requires expert input, leading to depth errors.
method: Propose DepthNet to predict target depth by learning the dielectric characteristics directly from raw B-scan data. DepthNet comprises three sub-nets: a dielectric prediction branch that ingests resized (224×224) B-scans with a ResNet101 encoder, and two fully connected branches that take Faster R-CNN bounding-box features; their outputs are fused to regress per-target depth with a weighted loss over dielectric and depth. This removes reliance on handcrafted dielectric assumptions and yields low average depth error, which is then used in migration for more accurate 3D reconstruction.
paper_title: GPR-based Subsurface Object Detection and Reconstruction Using Random Motion and DepthNet

problem: Projection-first deformable attention used in multi-view camera perception struggles to aggregate features across cameras because fixed 3D reference points often project outside certain images, and 2D sampling on the image plane causes depth-dependent scale inconsistency, hindering convergence and robustness.
method: Introduce a learning-first view attention that learns 3D sample regions per voxel query directly in 3D, defined in a query-specific local view coordinate (VC) system obtained by rotating around the z-axis by the query’s view angle. The learned 3D sample points are then projected to all cameras for attention-based aggregation, making feature collection data-driven and cross-camera while preserving consistent 3D scale and introducing rotation invariance around the vehicle. Implemented as multi-head attention over K learnable 3D offsets per head with normalization over cameras, it generalizes to map construction and 3D detection. Ablations show it outperforms projection-first deformable attention with higher accuracy (≈+1.2 mIoU, +0.43 IoUgeo) under similar compute.
paper_title: ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers

problem: Temporal modeling for multi-camera occupancy often relies on single-frame interaction or sliding-window training but online inference, causing train-test mismatch and extra computation; directly interacting all voxel queries with multi-frame histories is expensive and noisy due to many empty voxels.
method: Propose streaming temporal attention using an online BEV-level FIFO memory of multi-frame features with ego-motion compensation via feature warping. Voxel queries are squeezed into BEV queries for deformable-attention interaction with aligned memory frames and then unsqueezed back to voxels, reducing compute and denoising by operating on BEV cells. The BEV-to-BEV design improves occupancy and flow quality over voxel-to-BEV (≈+0.9 mIoU and notably lower mAVE) with negligible FPS overhead, and longer memory (N>1) further boosts performance. Consistent online streaming training also cuts training time versus sliding-window (≈36h→13h) while improving accuracy.
paper_title: ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers

problem: Existing flow supervision for occupancy is object-level, assigning a single center velocity to all voxels within an object and failing to capture fine-grained intra-object motion and background dynamics.
method: Construct FlowOcc3D by attaching occupancy-level flow vectors to Occ3D/nuScenes voxels, using object poses to rigidly map per-voxel centers across frames (p_{t-1}=O_{t-1}·O_t^{-1}·p_t) and compute per-voxel flow. This provides fine-grained supervision for dynamic scenes. Training ViewFormer with this supervision achieves state-of-the-art occupancy and flow on FlowOcc3D, outperforming strong baselines in mIoU/IoUgeo and reducing mAVE, and yields more realistic motion fields (e.g., distinct flows across different parts of turning vehicles).
paper_title: ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers

problem: Common activations like ReLU discard negative responses and create zero-gradient regions, and simple one-hidden-layer regressors with ReLUs lead to non-convex training; in contrast, low-dimensional regression often relies on linear splines.
method: Introduce a lifting layer that maps a scalar x to a higher-dimensional vector using ordered labels t1<...<tL (barycentric-like encoding). In its simplest form it behaves like two complementary ReLUs, avoiding zero-gradient plateaus. When followed by a fully connected linear layer, the network represents a continuous piecewise-linear (linear spline) function (Proposition 2) that can approximate any continuous function arbitrarily well (Corollary 3). With a convex loss, learning reduces to a convex optimization over linear parameters (Proposition 5). The construction extends to multidimensional inputs via vector-valued lifting on a triangulation or coordinate-wise lifting for high dimensions (Section 3.3).
paper_title: Lifting Layers: Analysis and Applications

problem: Robust training with non-convex or flat losses (e.g., truncated linear) is difficult and prone to local minima in standard networks.
method: Lift the network’s output (and the input to the loss) into a higher-dimensional space tailored to the loss; predict within the convex hull of the lifted range and map back via the inverse lifting. This converts minimization of a non-convex loss into a convex problem in the loss-layer parameters (Proposition 9), yielding a linear objective with simplex constraints and even a closed-form solution via per-column argmin over a cost matrix. The approach robustly fits data with heavy outliers, as demonstrated on a regression task with 40% outliers using a truncated linear loss (Example 10).
paper_title: Lifting Layers: Analysis and Applications

problem: Active vision research lacks a real-image dataset that lets agents simulate robot motion across dense viewpoints in indoor environments, and consistently label thousands of views for object instances.
method: Collect a dense RGB-D dataset of indoor scenes sampled on a 30 cm grid with 30° rotations (20k+ images), and define movement pointers (forward/back/left/right/rotate) to simulate robot actions. Obtain 2D instance boxes by labeling instances in 3D using COLMAP SfM plus CMVS/PMVS dense reconstructions, then project to each image; mitigate occlusions/noisy depths by aggregating neighboring depth maps and taking per-pixel minima with interpolation. Provide per-box difficulty metadata (e.g., size) and manually verify boxes.
paper_title: A Dataset for Developing and Benchmarking Active Vision

problem: State-of-the-art detectors are optimized for web-image biases and struggle with instance detection in robotic settings due to scale, viewpoint, and occlusion; additionally, it is unclear what spatial sampling is sufficient to emulate continuous motion.
method: Adapt SSD (VGG-based) for instance-level detection on the dataset, training on multiple scene splits and evaluating mAP for large vs. smaller boxes. Use the dense viewpoint coverage to analyze detection scores as a function of camera position, revealing consistent high/low-score regions tied to viewpoint/occlusion. Quantify sensitivity of detection scores to camera displacement from real robot videos, showing score changes grow with distance and that 30 cm steps approximate finer motions, justifying the dataset’s sampling resolution.
paper_title: A Dataset for Developing and Benchmarking Active Vision

problem: An agent must choose the next camera move to improve instance classification in cluttered scenes without explicit occlusion labels and under varying pose/scale.
method: Build an active recognition system with shared ResNet-18 features for an instance classifier and an action network. Pre-train the classifier on BigBIRD objects composited onto SUN397 backgrounds with strong augmentation (including large scale variation), then train the action network via REINFORCE to select among six actions (forward, backward, left, right, clockwise, counter-clockwise) over up to T steps, rewarding correct/high-confidence classifications. On the dataset, the learned policy consistently increases accuracy with more moves and outperforms random and always-forward baselines.
paper_title: A Dataset for Developing and Benchmarking Active Vision

problem: Weighted subsampling aims to approximate the full-set empirical risk minimizer, suffers from high variance due to inverse-probability weights, and by construction cannot produce a model that outperforms the full-set model, especially under distribution shift and label noise.
method: Propose Unweighted Influence Data Subsampling (UIDS), which selects a subset without inverse-probability weights by leveraging example-wise influence functions computed on a validation/test distribution to identify harmful training points. They show that making the perturbation/sampling rule a decreasing function of influence (i.e., negatively correlated with influence) guarantees the subset model’s test risk is no worse than, and can be better than, the full-set model (Lemma 2). This is operationalized by mapping small perturbations to sampling probabilities π(φ) and selecting data accordingly, enabling the subset-model to surpass the full-set model on out-of-sample tests.
paper_title: Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function

problem: Influence-based deterministic ‘dropout’ sampling can become over-confident on a specific validation distribution, hurting out-of-sample generalization under distribution shift.
method: Develop probabilistic sampling functions with bounded gradients to control confidence and improve robustness across distributions within a χ²-divergence ball around the empirical distribution. Theoretically, they prove the worst-case risk over this uncertainty set is Lipschitz in the influence vector with constant scaling as O(σ√((2δ+1)/n)), where σ bounds the sampling function’s gradient (Theorem 3), highlighting instability of deterministic dropout (unbounded gradient). They also introduce a surrogate confidence metric Γ(φ)=‖θ_ε−θ‖² and show it shares the same σ-dependent change rate (Theorem 4), and instantiate practical Linear and Sigmoid sampling functions that tune σ via a single parameter.
paper_title: Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function

problem: Computing influence functions at scale is challenging in high-dimensional, sparse settings due to expensive inverse Hessian–vector products (HVPs).
method: Use a Hessian-free mixed Preconditioned Conjugate Gradient (PCG) scheme to efficiently approximate inverse HVPs required by influence computation, combining an identity and Hessian-based preconditioner for faster convergence in ill-conditioned problems. This makes influence computation practical for large, sparse datasets (e.g., CTR tasks), with reported end-to-end IF computation times from seconds to a few minutes on million-scale feature spaces.
paper_title: Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function

problem: Single-frame object detectors degrade on videos due to deteriorated appearances (e.g., motion blur, defocus, rare poses), and prior video approaches mainly do box-level post-processing that is not end-to-end and does not improve feature quality.
method: Introduce Flow-Guided Feature Aggregation (FGFA), which aggregates features over neighboring frames at the feature level. For each reference frame, an optical flow network (e.g., FlowNet) estimates motion to nearby frames; their feature maps are bilinearly warped to the reference frame and fused via spatially varying adaptive weights computed from cosine similarity between learned embedding features of the warped neighbor and the reference. All modules—feature extractor, flow, embedding, warping, and detector—are trained end-to-end. On ImageNet VID, FGFA improves mAP from 73.4% to 76.3% and mAP on fast objects from 51.4% to 57.6%, while naive aggregation without flow or adaptive weights hurts performance; end-to-end training outperforms a non-joint variant.
paper_title: Flow-Guided Feature Aggregation for Video Object Detection

problem: Aggregating many frames during training is memory-intensive, making it hard to learn temporal aggregation effectively under GPU memory limits.
method: Use temporal dropout: during training, aggregate over a small number of randomly sampled neighboring frames (e.g., K=2) drawn from the same large temporal window used at inference, with weights normalized per location. This enables end-to-end training with limited memory while leveraging many frames at test time. Empirically, training with 2 vs. 5 frames yields similar accuracy, and mAP increases with more test frames, saturating around 21 frames (e.g., ResNet-50 mAP ≈ 74.0 at 21 frames).
paper_title: Flow-Guided Feature Aggregation for Video Object Detection

problem: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation trained with only image-level labels relies on sparse CAM seeds and off-the-shelf saliency maps that are used offline and not adapted to the target data, yielding low-quality pseudo labels and coarse boundaries.
method: AuxSegNet is a joint multi-task framework that treats semantic segmentation as the primary task and leverages multi-label image classification and saliency detection as auxiliary tasks, all sharing a backbone and trained end-to-end using only image-level labels and pre-trained saliency maps as initial pseudo labels. The classification branch produces CAMs; the saliency and segmentation branches use atrous conv decoders and are trained with losses on both initial and refined predictions against pseudo labels. Saliency pseudo labels are updated online by fusing the model’s refined saliency with pre-trained saliency through a dense CRF, while segmentation pseudo labels are generated from CAMs (and later their affinity-refined versions), enabling the auxiliary tasks to adapt to the target dataset and improve segmentation performance iteratively.
paper_title: Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation

problem: CAMs are incomplete and saliency cues are noisy; without modeling pixel-level relations across tasks, label propagation and boundary accuracy are limited in WSSS.
method: A cross-task global pixel affinity learning module learns pairwise affinities from both segmentation and saliency features using non-local self-attention blocks to produce task-specific HW×HW affinity matrices, then fuses them with a learned self-attention to obtain a cross-task affinity map. This map enhances features and refines both saliency and segmentation predictions via affinity-based propagation, and is also applied to CAMs to propagate activations for better pseudo segmentation labels. The affinity is trained under multi-task constraints by placing losses on both initial and affinity-refined predictions for saliency and segmentation, and the whole system is optimized in stage-wise iterative training where the learned affinity updates both saliency and segmentation pseudo labels after each stage, yielding progressive improvements (e.g., ablations show gains when moving from single-task to cross-task affinity and when enforcing both constraints).
paper_title: Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation

problem: Aerial tracking in cluttered environments needs fast generation of a feasible topology that keeps the target visible while guaranteeing safety, but existing planners either rely on heavy graph construction or fail under frequent occlusions and abrupt target motions.
method: Define an occlusion-free region Φ_k around each predicted target waypoint z_k and perform a hierarchical, greedy multi-goal path search that drives the drone to pass through Φ_1, Φ_2, … while rejecting occluded nodes and encouraging a desired tracking distance (III-A, lines 56–63). Using the resulting guiding path, generate a smart Safe Flight Corridor (SFC): start with a short segment along the drone’s initial velocity, then iteratively create polytopes to the intersection with the guiding path to reduce overlap and handle infeasible initial states (III-B, lines 64–76). This occlusion-aware path topology plus SFC provides a safe, visibility-respecting region for subsequent optimization with low computation.
paper_title: Elastic Tracker: A Spatio-temporal Trajectory Planner for Flexible Aerial Tracking

problem: Evaluating and enforcing visibility/occlusion constraints typically requires frequent ESDF construction, which is computationally expensive and limits real-time aerial tracking.
method: Avoid ESDF by analytically modeling visibility as sector-shaped regions V_k around each target waypoint z_k, seeded from a known visible point s_k and defined by a bisector ξ_k (III-C, lines 78–83). Use these sectors to impose occlusion-free constraints directly in the optimizer via absolute-time penalties with an angle clearance (IV-D, lines 184–191), as highlighted in the abstract’s contrast to ESDF-based methods (lines 29–35). This yields a lightweight occlusion cost that significantly reduces computation while preserving visibility.
paper_title: Elastic Tracker: A Spatio-temporal Trajectory Planner for Flexible Aerial Tracking

problem: Generating a spatio-temporal tracking trajectory that jointly ensures dynamic feasibility, collision avoidance, visibility, and proper viewing distance—while allocating time adaptively—often leads to discontinuous gradients or inconsistent formulations.
method: Adopt the T-MINCO polynomial trajectory class parameterized by intermediate waypoints q and piece durations T, and optimize a joint objective of minimum jerk and time with a slack T ≥ T_p (IV-A, lines 92–111; IV-B, lines 114–123). Enforce dynamic feasibility and collision avoidance using a relative-time integral penalty with trapezoidal quadrature for smooth, efficient gradients (IV-C, lines 132–149). Enforce visibility and distance at discrete prediction times via absolute-time penalties, using a C2 horizontal distance cost H_d and a tight vertical tolerance to balance safety and viewing quality (IV-D, lines 172–183, 184–191). Eliminate temporal inequality constraints by reparameterizing durations with τ (IV-E, lines 202–214), and rely on the MINCO mapping to keep gradients with respect to q and T continuous despite piece changes (IV-D, lines 168–171). This yields an efficient spatio-temporal optimizer that jointly handles safety and visibility within the SFC.
paper_title: Elastic Tracker: A Spatio-temporal Trajectory Planner for Flexible Aerial Tracking

problem: Language-guided mobile manipulation in novel environments requires predicting not only where to act but also how much force to apply; most prior goal predictors focus on kinematics alone, leading to failures in contact-rich tasks.
method: ForceSight is a text-conditioned RGBD vision transformer that predicts visual-force goals (kinematic + force) from a single eye-in-hand RGBD image and a natural-language prompt. It extends a ViT-L pre-trained on ImageNet-21k with an RGBD patch embedding (early fusion for depth) and injects a frozen T5 text embedding via cross-attention at every transformer block. The network outputs a 224×224 affordance map and a depth map to recover a 3D gripper target in the camera frame (argmax over affordance; masked L1 depth), and MLP heads predict yaw, gripper width, an applied-force vector, and grip force. Trained with weighted cross-entropy (affordance), masked L1 (depth), and MSE losses (forces/width/yaw), it achieves 0.036 m average fingertip error and 81% task success on 10 real-world tasks with unseen objects/environments; ignoring force goals drops success from 90% to 45% under matched starts, highlighting the benefit of force prediction.
paper_title: ForceSight: Text-Guided Mobile Manipulation with Visual-Force Goals

problem: Executing long-horizon, contact-rich tasks from an eye-in-hand perspective must be robust to calibration and visual ambiguities; visual-only servoing struggles without explicit force objectives.
method: A low-level controller performs closed-loop visual-force servoing toward the model’s visual-force goal, minimizing a joint objective over kinematic and force errors (with proportional gains and transformations) to issue incremental Cartesian end-effector and gripper motions, making the system insensitive to global calibration. Action primitives (e.g., approach, grasp, lift, pull, ungrasp) are appended to the text prompt and advanced via a simple state machine, enabling chaining of keyframes and error recovery. In real-robot ablations, removing forces reduces success to 50% over 20 trials and from 90% to 45% in paired trials; ablating depth, pre-training, augmentation, or text conditioning further degrades performance, evidencing the necessity of the joint visual-force objective and the overall system design.
paper_title: ForceSight: Text-Guided Mobile Manipulation with Visual-Force Goals

problem: Online video instance segmentation struggles to leverage temporal context effectively: prior online methods either fuse frames only at the pixel level or at the instance level via detection-and-crop RoIs, leading to incomplete, biased frame-to-frame communications and heavy per-frame instance matching.
method: Represent each frame with a hybrid pair of a global instance code (slot-based latent L×D capturing class and mask cues in an order-aware fashion) and CNN feature maps, and perform cropping-free temporal fusion via hybrid attentions. Intra-frame attention links code and pixels with code-to-code (c2c), code-to-pixel (c2p), and pixel-to-code (p2c) to build instance-aware features. Inter-frame attention fuses temporal information across adjacent frames using c2c&c2p to update target codes from reference codes/features and p2c to adjust pixel features, repeating intra- and inter-frame layers alternately within the backbone. This unified attention eliminates RoI cropping and enables instance-level aggregation directly from global context, yielding large gains (e.g., 41.3 mAP on YouTube-VIS 2019 with ResNet-50).
paper_title: Hybrid Instance-aware Temporal Fusion for Online Video Instance Segmentation

problem: Maintaining consistent instance identities online typically requires explicit frame-wise matching with handcrafted cues, which is brittle and computationally costly.
method: Make the instance code order-aware and enforce cross-frame slot consistency during training with an assignment loss that jointly matches class and mask similarity across current and previous frames (t and t−1) to retain slot order. At inference, assign identities directly by slot indices for non-empty predictions, with a simple IoU>0.5 fallback when slots shift, thus removing complex matching and post-processing. This matching-free identity association, enabled by learned slot consistency, achieves state-of-the-art performance on YouTube-VIS 2019/2021 (41.3/35.8 mAP).
paper_title: Hybrid Instance-aware Temporal Fusion for Online Video Instance Segmentation

problem: Global LiDAR–camera fusion often lacks precise cross-modal alignment and fuses over the whole scene, diluting object-relevant cues.
method: LoGoNet introduces a Global Fusion module with Centroid Dynamic Fusion (CDF) that represents each non-empty voxel by its point centroid rather than the voxel center to better match the true 3D surface geometry. These centroids are projected to the image plane and used as reference points for deformable cross-attention with learned offsets to aggregate nearby image features. The voxel features (queries) are fused with sampled image features (keys/values), concatenated and compressed via FFN, and ROI pooling over fused voxels produces proposal features for refinement.
paper_title: LoGoNet: Towards Accurate 3D Object Detection with Local-to-Global Cross-Modal Fusion

problem: Purely global fusion misses fine-grained, proposal-level geometry and context needed to detect small, distant, or occluded objects.
method: LoGoNet proposes a Local Fusion (LoF) module that divides each proposal into uniform 3D grids and encodes raw point cloud position information per grid via a Position Information Encoder (PIE), including relative grid location and local point statistics. Grid centers are projected to the image plane to sample local image features with learned offsets, and cross-attention fuses these samples with the encoded grid features. The result is a locally fused ROI-grid feature that captures fine-grained region-level multi-modal cues.
paper_title: LoGoNet: Towards Accurate 3D Object Detection with Local-to-Global Cross-Modal Fusion

problem: Features fused at global and local levels are computed independently and lack interaction, limiting their combined effectiveness.
method: LoGoNet adds a Feature Dynamic Aggregation (FDA) module that applies self-attention over the ROI-grid features to build relationships among grid points and enable interaction between globally and locally fused representations. A transformer encoder layer with residual connections aggregates these features into a more informative multi-modal representation, which is then used for second-stage bounding box refinement.
paper_title: LoGoNet: Towards Accurate 3D Object Detection with Local-to-Global Cross-Modal Fusion

problem: Temporal alignment across frames in multi-camera 3D perception is challenging, especially for DETR-style methods like PETR that implicitly encode 3D position into 2D features and thus lack explicit feature warping.
method: PETRv2 introduces 3D coordinates alignment (CA), transforming the 3D coordinates of the previous frame into the current frame’s coordinate system via pose transformation (using global coordinates as a bridge to compute T_{l(t)}^{l(t-1)}). The aligned 3D coordinates from frames t−1 and t are then used to generate position embeddings, and features/coordinates of adjacent frames are concatenated before decoding. This achieves temporal alignment through 3D PE rather than BEV feature warping and improves localization and velocity estimation (e.g., mAVE reduction).
paper_title: PETRv2: A Unified Framework for 3D Perception from Multi-Camera Images

problem: The original PETR 3D position embedding (3D PE) is data-independent, limiting adaptability and robustness because it does not leverage image content (e.g., depth cues).
method: PETRv2 proposes a feature-guided position encoder (FPE) that makes 3D PE data-dependent. Projected 2D features are passed through a small MLP and Sigmoid to produce attention weights that reweight the MLP-transformed 3D coordinates element-wise, yielding feature-guided 3D PEs. The 3D PE is added to 2D features to form keys, while projected 2D features serve as values for the transformer decoder. This improves detection metrics and robustness to extrinsics noise over PETR and PETR+FPE baselines.
paper_title: PETRv2: A Unified Framework for 3D Perception from Multi-Camera Images

problem: A unified framework for multi-task 3D perception (3D detection, BEV segmentation, 3D lane detection) from multi-camera images is needed without exploding query counts or changing architectures per task.
method: PETRv2 introduces sparse, task-specific queries initialized in task-appropriate spaces and decoded by a shared transformer: det queries in 3D space as learnable anchors; seg queries in BEV space where each query corresponds to a BEV patch (e.g., 25×25); and lane queries defined as 3D anchor lanes using fixed sampling along the Y-axis, predicting class, offsets (Δx, Δz) from anchors, and a visibility vector. This unified sparse-query design supports high-resolution BEV segmentation and accurate 3D lanes while keeping global attention tractable, achieving state-of-the-art results across tasks.
paper_title: PETRv2: A Unified Framework for 3D Perception from Multi-Camera Images

problem: Learning dexterous grasping directly from visual inputs is sample-inefficient, and naïve policy distillation from a privileged teacher fails to adapt when the student's visual observation space changes the feasible optimal behavior (e.g., self-occlusion breaks tracking).
method: Teacher-Augmented Policy Gradient (TAPG): a two-stage student–teacher framework. First, train a teacher with policy gradients on privileged low-dimensional states (oriented 3D bounding boxes). Then train a visual student on segmented point clouds with a combined objective: standard policy gradient plus a behavior cloning term gated by the teacher’s advantage/value, so the student imitates only when the teacher is estimated better. This permits deviation to handle visual constraints and comes with a performance guarantee under bounded imitation loss. TAPG learns perception-aware strategies and outperforms both visual RL and pure policy distillation, surpassing the teacher after adaptation.
paper_title: Grasp Anything: Combining Teacher-Augmented Policy Gradient Learning with Instance Segmentation to Grasp Arbitrary Objects

problem: Real-world deployment with promptable segmentation (e.g., SAM) can fail when grasps fully occlude small targets, causing segmentation/tracking loss; training with VFMs in-the-loop is impractical.
method: Perception-driven reward and sim-to-real pipeline: in simulation, use ground-truth instance masks and add an auxiliary reward that maximizes a target visibility ratio computed by projecting sampled surface points and counting those inside the mask, encouraging occlusion-benign grasps. At deployment, replace simulator masks with outputs from a promptable segmentation model (SAM) and apply hand dynamics calibration; this yields zero-shot transfer and robust promptable grasping, improving real-world success from 25% (policy distillation) to 55% (TAPG) across cluttered scenarios.
paper_title: Grasp Anything: Combining Teacher-Augmented Policy Gradient Learning with Instance Segmentation to Grasp Arbitrary Objects

problem: Monocular SLAM produces maps and trajectories at an arbitrary scale and suffers from time-varying scale drift, degrading metric accuracy.
method: Use a Bayesian filtering framework to estimate a time-varying local scale correction k_k online by fusing generic object detections with class-specific height priors and a motion model of scale drift. The transition p(k_k|k_{k-1}) is Gaussian with variance s_pk proportional to the accumulated camera rotation since the last update (clipped between s_min and s_max), capturing how drift grows with angular motion. When height priors are Gaussian, implement the update as a Kalman filter by marginalizing object height, yielding simple mean/variance prediction-correction steps.
paper_title: Bayesian Scale Estimation for Monocular SLAM Based on Generic Object Detection for Correcting Scale Drift

problem: Object detections can include background/occlusions, making naive height-from-bbox unreliable and leading to noisy scale observations.
method: Define a robust probabilistic observation model that estimates object height from SLAM map points projected inside the detection. Compute a representative 3D point on the object surface by gamma-weighted averaging of depth-ranked points, construct a vertical 3D line through it, and intersect it with back-projected rays from the top/bottom of the detection to get height Ĥ. Form the scale observation as ĸ̂=H/Ĥ and model the likelihood as Gaussian around k_k with standard deviation derived via uncertainty propagation from the depth variance of the selected points.
paper_title: Bayesian Scale Estimation for Monocular SLAM Based on Generic Object Detection for Correcting Scale Drift

problem: Need a practical, real-time integration that corrects scale drift during operation and demonstrably improves trajectory accuracy.
method: Integrate the Bayesian/Kalman scale estimator into ORB-SLAM2, using YOLO9000 detections and a Gaussian prior on car height (mean ~1.5 m). Run updates periodically in real time with negligible overhead. On KITTI, this reduces average relative translation error to ~5.5%, outperforming object-detection-based baselines and showing a large gain over a variant without the motion model (≈3× improvement over prior work).
paper_title: Bayesian Scale Estimation for Monocular SLAM Based on Generic Object Detection for Correcting Scale Drift

problem: Real-world robotic reinforcement learning is sample-inefficient due to difficult exploration and heavy reliance on costly human demonstrations.
method: SimLauncher pre-trains a visuomotor policy in a digital-twin simulator and uses its rollouts to accelerate real-world RL. It bootstraps the critic in a hybrid off-policy RL setup (RLPD) with demonstrations from both simulated rollouts and limited real-world rollouts of the pre-trained policy, sampling 25% D_sim, 25% D_real, and 50% online replay per batch. During interaction, it proposes actions by comparing the BC (pre-trained) action and the RL policy action via a Boltzmann distribution over Q-values (p ∝ exp(βQ)), and uses the selected action for bootstrap targets. This combination substantially improves sample efficiency and achieves near-perfect success across multi-stage and dexterous tasks compared to IBRL/RLPD baselines.
paper_title: SimLauncher: Launching Sample-Efficient Real-world Robotic Reinforcement Learning via Simulation Pre-training

problem: Transferring visuomotor policies from simulation to the real world is hindered by visual/physical sim-to-real gaps, and critics can overfit if trained only on simulation data.
method: The framework builds task-specific digital twins and trains policies via a privileged-to-visual distillation pipeline: learn a state-based (privileged) policy with RL in sim, generate successful trajectories, then behavior clone a visuomotor policy using RGB + proprio. It mitigates sim-to-real gaps by calibrating physics/cameras, randomizing camera pose in sim demos, applying visual augmentations during BC, and masking backgrounds at train/test time with SAM2-tracked segmentations. A small number of real rollouts from the pre-trained policy are added to the demo buffer and sampled equally with simulated demos to regularize the critic and prevent value underestimation on real transitions.
paper_title: SimLauncher: Launching Sample-Efficient Real-world Robotic Reinforcement Learning via Simulation Pre-training

problem: Success-only simulated demonstrations may provide limited state coverage, weakening bootstrapping for real-world RL.
method: SimLauncher augments demonstration coverage by constructing hybrid demos: it uniformly collects rollouts throughout state-based policy training and post-renders image observations, yielding broader coverage of intermediate and failure states. Experiments show simulated demos alone can effectively bootstrap real-world RL, and that increasing simulated demo scale or using hybrid demos further improves sample efficiency.
paper_title: SimLauncher: Launching Sample-Efficient Real-world Robotic Reinforcement Learning via Simulation Pre-training

problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
method: PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).
paper_title: TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model

problem: There is a lack of high-fidelity simulators for non-optical tactile sensors, hindering simulation-driven policy learning and data generation.
method: DigiTac is a digital twin built via a Real2Sim pipeline: an FEM-based elastomer model is created in Isaac Gym (soft-body mesh with 7636 vertices), and synchronized real/sim data are collected (FEM node positions/stresses X, simulated force F_sim, real MEMS outputs S, real force F_real). Dynamic Time Warping aligns real and simulated sequences. To map FEM outputs to sensor readings, the method aggregates Von Mises stress features from a 609-node subset located above the 8 taxels and feeds them to an MLP trained with L1 loss (AdamW, noise injection). The model reproduces PhysTac with an average error of 5.95% of the maximum contact pressure. (Supported: lines 80–87, 94–107, 110–123, 116–121, 152).
paper_title: TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model

problem: Learning from limited real tactile data yields poor performance for downstream tasks like object/indenter classification.
method: Using DigiTac, the authors synthesize time-series tactile signals by varying indentation depths, speeds, and rotation angles, and train a time-series Transformer classifier on hybrid data. With 7 indenters, combining 20 real trials and 96 simulated trials per indenter in training (testing on 20 real trials) boosts accuracy from 33.57% (real-only) to 95.0% (hybrid), demonstrating effective Sim2Real augmentation by the digital twin. (Supported: lines 156–183).
paper_title: TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model

problem: Pointwise LLM rerankers trained as binary classifiers (yes/no) suffer from poor score discrimination, especially with reasoning LLMs whose CoT makes predictions overconfident and saturated, harming ranking quality.
method: Reformulate reranking as a generative fine-grained scoring task: the model outputs an integer relevance score from 0–10 with a Chain-of-Thought, and the final ranking score is computed as s_i × Pr(token = s_i). Train this via SFT with a structured prompt to leverage the LLM’s autoregressive capabilities, yielding more expressive, better-separated scores and consistent nDCG@10 gains across benchmarks.
paper_title: ERANK: Fusing Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning for Effective and Efficient Text Reranking

problem: Efficient pointwise rerankers lack global listwise awareness, while listwise/setwise RL training is effective but incurs prohibitive latency and memory for real-world deployment.
method: Adopt a two-stage pipeline that adds RL with GRPO to an SFT-tuned pointwise model, using a novel listwise-derived, rule-based reward r_RR. For each query, aggregate N×G scores from all documents and rollouts, sort them, and compute rewards that: (a) give positives high reciprocal-rank rewards, (b) penalize negatives ranked above any positive, and (c) give correctly-below negatives a smooth squared-error reward against the SFT reference score. Use KL regularization to stabilize policy updates. This injects global ranking signals into a pointwise architecture while preserving low-latency inference.
paper_title: ERANK: Fusing Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning for Effective and Efficient Text Reranking

problem: High-quality, stable, fine-grained supervision for both semantic and reasoning-intensive relevance is scarce; single-pass teacher labels can be noisy/unstable.
method: Synthesize an SFT dataset using a strong teacher (QwQ-32B) to generate CoT and integer scores over diverse sources (MS MARCO, ReasonIR HQ, Promptriever). Enrich negatives by retrieving top-1,000 candidates with ReasonIR-8B and sampling hard/medium/easy negatives to 20 docs/query. For each pair, perform multiple generations and select the sample closest to the consensus (average) score, improving label stability and nDCG; filter long outputs. This produces a high-quality dataset that trains nuanced, robust fine-grained scoring.
paper_title: ERANK: Fusing Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning for Effective and Efficient Text Reranking

problem: It is unclear what step-level reward models (SRMs) actually reward in mathematical reasoning and whether natural language chain-of-thought is necessary for effective process supervision.
method: The authors formalize math reasoning as an MDP with state=(thoughts T, equations E) and action=next thought, and run an AlphaZero-like MCTS to collect step-level preference pairs using two LLM roles: an agent to propose next thoughts and a world model to execute thoughts into equations (Llama-3-8B-Instruct for both), with n=6 candidates and 500 iterations per problem; correctness is verified by an SFT model (DeepSeek-Math-7B-Base). They then train ablated SRMs—Full-Context (FC-SRM), Math-Only (MO-SRM), Single-Step Math-Only (SSMO-SRM), and Next-Thought (NT-SRM)—on DeepSeek-Math-7B-Base and Qwen2-7B, and use them as scoring functions in greedy/beam search (c=5, B=1). Math-only SRMs match or slightly outperform full-context SRMs (e.g., GSM8K: 85.82 vs 86.20; MATH: 39.64 vs 38.58 with DeepSeek base), and t-tests show no significant differences (all p>0.05), indicating natural language thought descriptions are unnecessary for effective SRMs.
paper_title: What Are Step-Level Reward Models Rewarding? Counterintuitive Findings from MCTS-boosted Mathematical Reasoning

problem: Do SRMs capture multi-step logical coherence in mathematical reasoning, and can they do so from natural language descriptions alone?
method: To isolate cross-step coherence, the paper compares MO-SRM (scores using the full history of equations) against SSMO-SRM (scores using only the current step equation). MO-SRM yields substantially larger gains than SSMO-SRM (DeepSeek base: +7.35%/+8.48% vs +3.64%/+6.30% on GSM8K/MATH; Qwen2 base shows similar trends), showing SRMs learn logical coherence across steps from mathematical language. In contrast, NT-SRM, which evaluates the next thought’s natural language given the current context, is hard to train (training curves stagnate) and delivers weak gains near baseline (e.g., +0.91% on GSM8K, −0.18% on MATH with DeepSeek base), indicating SRMs struggle to assess logical coherence from natural language descriptions alone.
paper_title: What Are Step-Level Reward Models Rewarding? Counterintuitive Findings from MCTS-boosted Mathematical Reasoning

problem: Practitioners need guidance on whether to invest in RL for sequential course recommendation or rely on simpler heuristics when optimizing job applicability at scale.
method: They empirically compare exhaustive, greedy, and RL (DQN, PPO) on open datasets (IT-focused jobs, Udemy courses, and resumes) using two metrics: average number of applicable jobs after k recommended courses and average recommendation time. Results show greedy yields the highest rewards for short sequences (k=1–3), while PPO maintains comparable reward with substantially faster execution for longer sequences (k=4–5). Complexity analysis further supports RL’s scalability when the skill space is much smaller than the job set (RL O(k|S||C|) vs. greedy O(k|J||C|)).
paper_title: Course Recommender Systems Need to Consider the Job Market

problem: Large-scale human motion datasets contain many movements that are dynamically infeasible for real humanoids due to embodiment and dynamics gaps, which degrades training of whole-body imitators.
method: H2O introduces a sim-to-data pipeline that first retargets AMASS motions to the humanoid by (i) fitting an SMPL body shape β′ to the robot using 12 corresponding joints and (ii) optimizing end-effector/keypoint positions (12 joints) via Adam to preserve motion patterns. It then trains a privileged motion imitator (PHC/PULSE-style) with full rigid-body states and global goals, without domain randomization, to imitate all retargeted sequences and filters out any the policy cannot track, yielding a humanoid-feasible dataset (≈8.5k/10k sequences). This uses simulation as an automatic feasibility oracle to clean data at scale.
paper_title: Learning Human-to-Humanoid Real-Time Whole-Body Teleoperation

problem: Enabling real-time, whole-body humanoid teleoperation from only an RGB camera requires a controller that uses only real-world-available signals and is robust to sim-to-real gaps while precisely tracking diverse human motions.
method: H2O trains a goal-conditioned RL policy with a deployment-ready state: proprioception (joint positions/velocities, root linear/angular velocities, projected gravity, last action) and motion goals from eight 3D keypoints (shoulders, elbows, hands, ankles) including positions, robot-relative offsets, and velocities, all in the robot frame. The reward provides dense full-body tracking terms; robustness is achieved via extensive domain randomization (e.g., friction, link masses, torso CoM offset, PD gains, torque noise, control delay, terrains, external pushes) and safety regularization/early terminations. Deployed zero-shot on a Unitree H1, the policy tracks 3D human poses estimated by HybrIK at 30 Hz from a single RGB webcam, executing dynamic whole-body behaviors (walking, kicking, jumping, pick-and-place). In simulation ablations, H2O outperforms reduced/uncleaned variants (e.g., 72.5% success vs. 67.9% and 53.2%; lower MPJPE/vel/acc errors).
paper_title: Learning Human-to-Humanoid Real-Time Whole-Body Teleoperation

problem: Do Gradient TD policy-evaluation algorithms (GTD, GTD2, TDC) remain stable and convergent when augmented with heavy-ball momentum to accelerate learning?
method: Introduce heavy-ball momentum variants (GTD-M, GTD2-M, TDC-M) with a time-varying momentum η_t that approaches 1, chosen to enable a decomposition of the momentum-augmented updates into three coupled linear iterates. In the one-timescale setting (equal step-sizes), show the combined driving matrix G is Hurwitz under the condition w(w+1) > ||Â||^2 (e.g., satisfied by w≥1 with bounded features/rewards), and then use the ODE method to prove the iterates are stable and converge almost surely to the TD solution θ* = −Â^{-1} b̄. (Supported by Lemma 1 and Theorem 2.)
paper_title: Gradient Temporal Difference with Momentum: Stability and Convergence

problem: Existing stochastic approximation theory lacks general stability and convergence guarantees for three-timescale recursions, which arise from the momentum-based decomposition of Gradient TD.
method: Develop the first general stability and almost sure convergence conditions for three-timescale stochastic approximation via an ODE framework with timescale separation and scaled limiting ODEs (assumptions B1–B7; Theorem 3). Apply this to the GTD-M/2-M/TDC-M decompositions with appropriately separated step-sizes to establish almost sure convergence to the same TD solution for any w>0 (Theorem 4 and Appendix A3), relaxing the restrictive one-timescale condition on w.
paper_title: Gradient Temporal Difference with Momentum: Stability and Convergence

problem: Assess whether momentum-augmented GTD variants yield practical improvements in policy evaluation performance.
method: Evaluate GTD-M, GTD2-M, and TDC-M (one- and three-timescale settings) on standard benchmarks (Boyan Chain, 5- and 19-state Random Walks, Random MDP) using RMSPBE. Empirically observe consistent improvements over vanilla algorithms, with the three-timescale schedule allowing smaller w (larger initial effective momentum) and faster early error reduction. (Section 5; Figures 1–4; summary at lines 317–318.)
paper_title: Gradient Temporal Difference with Momentum: Stability and Convergence

problem: Offline policy learning with softmax policies over million-scale item catalogs is computationally prohibitive: each SGD step is O(P) due to the softmax normalizer and expectations/sampling over all actions.
method: They derive a covariance-form policy gradient that removes the need to compute the softmax normalizing constant, then approximate the remaining expectations via self-normalized importance sampling. The proposal q_{ε,K} mixes a uniform distribution with a distribution over the approximate top-K actions under the current policy, where top-K is obtained by a prebuilt approximate MIPS index (HNSW/FAISS) over item embeddings. Algorithm 1 samples S actions from q, computes normalized weights ω=exp{f(a,x)}/q(a|x), and forms a weighted covariance between the reward estimator r̂(a,x) and ∇θfθ(a,x) to update θ. This turns each iteration from O(P) to O(log P + K), scaling logarithmically with catalog size while maintaining policy quality, yielding 5–25× (up to 30×) speedups over REINFORCE in experiments with equal or better rewards.
paper_title: Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation

problem: Serving-time constraints require millisecond top-1 retrieval from large catalogs, and training–serving mismatch or learning full item embeddings can hurt stability and performance.
method: They parameterize the policy with a dot-product form fθ(a,x)=hθ(x)ᵀβa and assume fixed item embeddings β, learning only the user transform hθ(x). A single approximate MIPS index over β is built once and reused both at inference (argmax retrieval) and during training to construct q_{ε,K}, aligning optimization with deployment. Empirically, fixing β has negligible training-time cost and improves performance versus training β (Table 2), supporting the design choice.
paper_title: Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation

problem: Unlabeled target data in the wild is a compound of multiple latent domains with no domain labels, making standard domain adaptation struggle to align and generalize across mixed, heterogeneous distributions.
method: Train a class-discriminative encoder E_class on labeled source data and learn a separate domain encoder E_domain via a class-confusion objective that removes class information (a discriminator is trained to predict class labels while the domain encoder is trained to force randomized class predictions) with a reconstruction constraint to ensure completeness.
