Use the learned E_domain to compute per-instance domain gaps as the mean L2 distance from a target sample’s domain feature to source domain features, and rank targets from easy (closest) to hard (farthest). Perform curriculum domain adaptation by progressively including farther target instances; at each stage optimize source supervised cross-entropy and a domain-adversarial confusion loss between source and the current target subset, growing robustness to the entire compound target.
paper_title: Open Compound Domain Adaptation

problem: At inference, inputs may arrive from open (previously unseen) domains where direct image features are unreliable, degrading classification.
method: Introduce a memory module M that stores source class prototypes (centroids). For each input, form a class enhancer v_enhance as a softmax-weighted combination of prototypes based on the direct feature v_direct, and compute a domain indicator e_domain = T(E_domain(x)) (a lightweight tanh network over domain features) to gauge how far the input is from the source. Build a domain-aware representation by dynamically balancing v_direct and v_enhance using e_domain, then apply a cosine classifier with L2-normalized features. This memory-augmented, domain-aware fusion transfers source knowledge when needed and improves agility on open domains without sacrificing performance on the compound target.
paper_title: Open Compound Domain Adaptation

problem: End-to-end RL for LLM agents jointly optimizes planning and summarization, causing competing objectives and difficult credit assignment; moreover, scarce verifiable data yields weak or noisy rewards, making it hard to improve the agent’s planning.
method: RLTR decouples training to optimize only the Planner. It cold-starts the Planner via knowledge distillation and rejection sampling from a teacher LLM, then applies multi-turn RL with a KL-regularized objective to improve tool-use actions. The reward directly targets planning quality: a verification LLM computes tool-use completeness by checking the action trajectory with a binary completeness function averaged over N samples, and rule-based penalties add format-invalid (−1), repetition, and tool-error penalties. During training, tool-use results are masked from loss computation to avoid gradient dilution. The optimized, modular Planner can pair with any Summarizer and yields 8–12% higher tool-use completeness and 5–6% better final responses than end-to-end baselines.
paper_title: Encouraging Good Processes Without the Need for Good Answers: Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agent Planning

problem: Final-answer-based reward models are unreliable on the predominant non-verifiable industrial data and are prone to reward hacking, leaving most data without effective RL signals.
method: Replace final-answer rewards with a tool-use completeness reward that evaluates whether the action sequence includes all necessary tool calls and steps, computed by a verification LLM and averaged over multiple samples to stabilize scoring. This reward aligns better with human labels than answer-based rewards (ACC 74.59% vs 65.30%, F1 84.64% vs 76.17%) and enables stable training. The framework is algorithm-agnostic, working with PPO, GRPO, and REINFORCE++, and consistently outperforms end-to-end RL baselines under the same environment.
paper_title: Encouraging Good Processes Without the Need for Good Answers: Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agent Planning

problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
method: HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.
paper_title: Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control

problem: Using options as policy building blocks can lead to suboptimal final behavior and sensitivity to the chosen option set; model-based rollouts can suffer from model bias.
method: Use skills only to guide exploration—not to constrain the learned policy—so the final policy remains over primitive actions. Empirically, this look-ahead exploration learns faster and reaches higher success rates than HER, parameterized action space (PAS), hierarchical HER-PAS, and parameter-space noise on Baxter manipulation tasks (pick-and-move, put-in, stack, take-out). The approach remains effective under model mismatch (e.g., an unseen wall) and with reduced skill sets, though learning slows when essential skills like grasp are missing.
paper_title: Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control

problem: Learning effective twists that capture long-horizon target information is challenging without access to exact target marginals over prefixes.
method: Propose Contrastive Twist Learning (CTL), a new objective that minimizes the sum over timesteps of KL(σ(s_{1:t}) || π_θ,t(s_{1:t})), yielding an energy-based gradient E_{σ(s_{1:t})}[∇θ log ψ_θ,t] − E_{π_θ,t}[∇θ log ψ_θ,t]. CTL supports multiple positive-sampling regimes: exact target samples via BDMC or rejection sampling when available, and approximate positives via SIS/SMC reweighting otherwise; negatives are estimated with SIS/SMC under base or twist-induced proposals. This mass-covering marginal-matching trains twists to avoid premature pruning and aligns with density-ratio/EBM principles.
paper_title: Probabilistic Inference in Language Models via Twisted Sequential Monte Carlo

problem: There is no general, quantitative way to assess how closely an inference method (e.g., PPO, proposal policies, or SMC samplers) matches the desired sequence-level target distribution.
method: Develop bidirectional SMC bounds on the log partition function: a standard SMC/IWAE-style stochastic lower bound and a novel SMC stochastic upper bound that becomes computable given a single exact target sample, using an extended state-space construction that guarantees cloning of the exact sample during resampling. The bound gap equals KLs on the extended space, yielding tight sandwiching with larger K and providing upper bounds on KL(q_SMC||σ) or KL(σ||q_SMC) by data processing. These bounds enable estimating both D_KL(q||σ) and D_KL(σ||q) for arbitrary proposals and diagnosing behaviors like mode dropping.
paper_title: Probabilistic Inference in Language Models via Twisted Sequential Monte Carlo

problem: In continuous-action RL, maximizing Q over actions is approximate and unstable, and naive uniform discretization suffers from the curse of dimensionality; moreover, human demonstrations are often multimodal so standard Behavioral Cloning collapses to a single mode.
method: AQuaDem learns a state-dependent discretization Ψ(s) that outputs K candidate continuous actions by minimizing a soft-min reconstruction loss over demonstrations, with a temperature T controlling how many candidates must fit each demo action (multimodality). This turns the original continuous MDP into a discrete one with K actions per state, enabling exact argmax over Q with standard discrete-action RL. The learned candidates are smooth and state-dependent (visualized in gridworlds), and ablations show that state-independent or random candidate sets fail (zero success across tasks).
paper_title: Continuous Control with Action Quantization from Demonstrations

problem: Leveraging demonstrations or play data to efficiently solve sparse, high-dimensional robotic manipulation tasks remains challenging for both RL with demonstrations and imitation learning.
method: Instantiate AQuaDem in two stages: (1) learn Ψ(s) offline from demonstrations or play; (2) train a discrete RL/IL algorithm over the quantized actions. In RL with demonstrations (AQuaDQN), use Munchausen DQN with two replay buffers (env and demos sampled at a fixed ratio), discretizing demo actions by nearest candidate; it outperforms SAC and SACfD in success rate and sample efficiency on Adroit Door, Pen, Hammer (1M steps). In imitation learning (AQuaGAIL), run GAIL with a discriminator and Munchausen DQN over quantized actions, matching or exceeding human success and achieving lower Wasserstein state-distribution distance than GAIL/BC/MDN. With play data (AQuaPlay), learn Ψ(s) from unlabeled play and train task-specific discrete RL, consistently outperforming SAC on Robodesk while naive 'bang-bang' discretization degrades with finer grids.
paper_title: Continuous Control with Action Quantization from Demonstrations

problem: Offline RL methods like CQL in continuous action spaces require complex sampling to estimate the log-sum-exp regularizer and are sensitive to out-of-distribution actions.
method: Use AQuaDem to discretize actions and apply a discrete CQL objective (AQuaCQL), where the log-sum-exp term is exact over the K candidates. On D4RL locomotion tasks, AQuaCQL attains competitive scores with TD3+BC, CQL, and IQL, showing that discretization from demos can simplify offline RL while preserving performance.
paper_title: Continuous Control with Action Quantization from Demonstrations

problem: Unsupervised abstractive sentence summarization lacks reference summaries, making it hard to optimize for content preservation and fluency, and existing abstractive methods underperform extractive ones while offering limited control over output length.
method: Formulate summarization as a Markov decision process and train a pretrained seq2seq policy (T5) with self-critical policy gradient using reward signals only. Combine three rewards: (i) content preservation via semantic similarity between sentence embeddings of input and summary (cosine over sent2vec/SBERT, normalized to [0,1]); (ii) fluency via a language-model perplexity score (GPT-2 fine-tuned on headlines) mapped to a smooth reward; and (iii) length control by prepending a target-length prompt to the input and rewarding adherence to that length. This enables fully unsupervised, differentiability-free optimization of abstractive summaries with controllable length.
paper_title: Generating Multiple-Length Summaries via Reinforcement Learning for Unsupervised Sentence Summarization

problem: Generating high-quality summaries at multiple target lengths is unevenly difficult (shorter summaries are harder), and training each length independently ignores useful cross-length signals.
method: Introduce Multi-Summary Learning (MSL): for each input, generate summaries for several target lengths and add a mutual-quality reward that makes a target summary semantically similar to other summaries that are both higher-quality and closer in length. Use a usefulness function that upweights pairs when the other summary’s quality (from content-preservation and fluency rewards) exceeds the target’s and when their lengths are close, and apply this as a weighted semantic-similarity term during RL training. This cross-length coupling improves especially shorter-length summaries without adding inference-time cost.
paper_title: Generating Multiple-Length Summaries via Reinforcement Learning for Unsupervised Sentence Summarization

problem: RL training from scratch is unstable and slow, and existing generic pretraining (e.g., denoising) does not teach sentence-level summarization or explicit length control.
method: Pretrain with a prompt-based text reconstruction task: perturb inputs by shuffling, dropping, and adding words, prepend the original text length as a prompt (e.g., '20:'), and train the model to reconstruct the clean text. This teaches reordering, deletion/addition, and explicit length control before RL. Initializing RL with this pretraining accelerates optimization (notably the length reward) and improves final summary quality.
paper_title: Generating Multiple-Length Summaries via Reinforcement Learning for Unsupervised Sentence Summarization

problem: Design a reinforcement learning algorithm that is safe at every step under instantaneous hard constraints when both states and actions can be unsafe, in episodic linear mixture MDPs where ergodicity breaks and unsafe transitions must be avoided.
method: Introduce LSVI-NEW, which guarantees per-step safety by constructing safe state-action subgraphs in a backward manner. At step h, it declares (s,a) safe only if (i) all state-action-next-state triplets satisfy the instantaneous constraint and (ii) every possible next state belongs to the safe set at h+1. Safety values are estimated via a projected regularized least-squares estimator onto U⊥ with an explicit UCB term (Equations (7)–(8)), and action selection is restricted to the resulting safe sets. This look-ahead construction ensures that all future states reachable from any chosen (s,a) remain safe (Theorem 1).
paper_title: A NEAR-OPTIMAL ALGORITHM FOR SAFE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING UNDER INSTANTANEOUS HARD CONSTRAINTS

problem: Safety constraints restrict exploration and create strong coupling across steps, so the learner must explore optimistically without violating constraints while handling uncertainty in transitions and in both future and past subgraphs.
method: Augment Q-value estimates with three novel optimism bonuses (Step-4, Eq. (13)) tailored to the safe-subgraph setting: (i) a max-over-next-states transition-uncertainty bonus to prioritize the most uncertain transition for (s,a) (Idea II, Eq. (14)); (ii) a max-over-future-subgraphs bonus to encourage exploring downstream regions with higher uncertainty (Idea III, Eq. (15)); and (iii) a bonus capturing uncertainty propagated from earlier steps by peeking backward (Idea IV, Eq. (16)). Combined with a standard Hoeffding bonus, these resolve the pessimism–optimism trade-off induced by safety and enable sublinear regret while remaining safe.
paper_title: A NEAR-OPTIMAL ALGORITHM FOR SAFE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING UNDER INSTANTANEOUS HARD CONSTRAINTS

problem: Provide near-optimal theoretical guarantees for safe RL under instantaneous hard constraints in non-ergodic linear mixture MDPs, where standard optimistic V-value invariants fail, and show the necessity of dependence on a safety-gap parameter.
method: Develop new value-function constructions and invariants that hold even when optimal and learned policies visit different state sets (Lemma 1), and quantitatively bound impacts from future and past steps (Lemmas 2–3) to calibrate the new bonus terms. Prove high-probability safety (Theorem 1) and a regret upper bound that scales as ˜O(d H^3 √(dK) / Δ_c) matching the unsafe-actions-only setting and nearly the unconstrained case (Theorem 2), and establish a lower bound showing the dependence on the safety parameter Δ_c is necessary (Theorem 3).
paper_title: A NEAR-OPTIMAL ALGORITHM FOR SAFE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING UNDER INSTANTANEOUS HARD CONSTRAINTS

problem: LLM-based theorem provers often generate syntactically invalid or inapplicable tactics, and standard supervised training does not match the inference-time beam search with verifier feedback. RL approaches that use trajectory-level rewards are expensive and suffer from sparse signals.
method: LeanListener brings the verifier (Lean) into the training loop with single-step, local look-ahead guidance. For each proof state, it samples multiple tactics via beam search and uses Lean feedback to compute a per-step reward that captures tactic applicability and local progress: R(a_t; s_t) = softplus(G(s_t) − G(s_{t+1})), assigning 0 to invalid tactics. The policy is optimized with GRPO, using group-normalized advantages and KL regularization without a separate critic, and trained online by periodically updating the sampling policy every 50 iterations. This step-wise verifier-in-the-loop alignment avoids full-trajectory supervision while aligning training with inference-time proof-tree expansion.
paper_title: Local Look-Ahead Guidance via Verifier-in-the-Loop for Automated Theorem Proving

problem: Static pairwise preference optimization (e.g., DPO) with binary applicable/inapplicable labels can increase valid tactic rate but may not translate into stronger theorem proving, and offline data generation fails to sufficiently improve tactic quality.
method: The paper replaces binary, static preferences with online GRPO using Lean-derived local progress rewards, enabling on-policy exploration of new, applicable tactics beyond human labels. Empirically, online GRPO increases valid-tactic precision@8 from 40.77 (ReProver) to 51.01 and reduces zero-precision steps from 12.74% to 7.44, while improving Pass@1 on LeanDojo (random: 53.21 vs. 52.76; novel-premises: 41.11 vs. 40.86). It also speeds inference (up to ~20% less time to solve the same number of theorems) and yields shorter proofs, showing that local look-ahead rewards and online training outperform offline DPO.
paper_title: Local Look-Ahead Guidance via Verifier-in-the-Loop for Automated Theorem Proving

problem: Implicit-feedback recommender models trained with classic point-wise or pair-wise losses inherit popularity bias because observed interactions conflate true relevance with exposure propensities of active users and popular items.
method: Introduce Fair Sampling (FS), a sampling-level training scheme that, for every training instance, constructs supplementary samples so each user and each item are equally likely to appear as both positive and negative. Two variants are provided: FS-Point for point-wise losses and FS-Pair for pair-wise losses. FS-Point augments each (u,i,y) with complementary user–item samples that flip labels to balance appearances; FS-Pair augments each positive–negative pair (u,i;j) with a crossed pair (ũ,j,i) under non-interaction constraints. This cancels the optimization of user/item propensity factors in the loss and removes the need to estimate propensity scores, thereby mitigating popularity bias.
paper_title: Unbiased Collaborative Filtering with Fair Sampling

problem: Existing IPS-based debiasing relies on estimating propensity scores, which is difficult in practice and can introduce high variance or residual bias; a principled alternative is needed that targets the source of bias in the objectives themselves.
method: Provide a theoretical analysis that decomposes exposure probability into user propensity, item propensity, and relevance, then shows classic point-wise and pair-wise objectives implicitly optimize propensity terms (e.g., ln φ_u and ln φ_i), biasing models toward popular items. Prove that the FS constructions (FS-Point and FS-Pair) offset these propensity terms in the optimization—so only the relevance component is optimized—achieving unbiased learning without propensity estimation.
paper_title: Unbiased Collaborative Filtering with Fair Sampling

problem: To make importance sampling practical at scale, one needs a proposal distribution over actions that is both fast to sample/evaluate and close to the target policy; constructing such a proposal without incurring O(P) costs is challenging.
method: Fix the item embedding matrix and learn only the user/context transform, enabling a pre-built approximate MIPS index over items. Construct a proposal q_{ε,K} as a mixture of a uniform distribution and a distribution supported on the top-K actions returned by approximate MIPS for the current context. This yields O(log P) sampling (to retrieve top-K) and O(K) evaluation for weights, keeps the proposal close to the policy across training phases via ε, and enables a fast SGD algorithm (Algorithm 1) that achieves order-of-magnitude training speedups (5–25×, up to 30×) with equal or better policy quality on large-scale datasets.
paper_title: Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation

problem: There is no clear, job-market-aware metric or matching scheme to judge whether a user can apply to jobs or should enroll in a course, hindering alignment and explainability.
method: Define axiomatic similarity/relevance functions: a user–job similarity that averages min(user level, job level)/job level over required skills to count applicable jobs, and a user–course relevance that combines required-skill match with the complement of provided-skill match to avoid recommending courses teaching only known skills. Thresholds on these functions operationalize course enrollability and job applicability and underpin both filtering and the reward used in JCRec.
paper_title: Course Recommender Systems Need to Consider the Job Market

problem: In sequential recommendation, representing a user with a single embedding cannot capture multiple concurrent interests, and many approaches underuse the sequential structure of behavior histories.
method: ComiRec introduces a multi-interest extraction module that generates K interest embeddings per user from their behavior sequence. It provides two realizations: (1) ComiRec-DR applies the original CapsNet dynamic routing on item-behavior embeddings (with a bilinear transformation) to form interest capsules that better preserve sequential information; and (2) ComiRec-SA uses multi-head self-attention with trainable positional embeddings to produce diverse interest vectors. During training, the interest closest (via argmax) to the target item is selected and optimized with sampled softmax. At serving, each interest independently retrieves top-N items from a large pool via fast nearest-neighbor search (e.g., Faiss), yielding K·N candidates.
paper_title: Controllable Multi-Interest Framework for Recommendation

problem: Merging candidates from multiple interests often maximizes accuracy but can produce monotonous lists with low per-user diversity. A controllable trade-off between accuracy and diversity is needed at aggregation.
method: ComiRec proposes a controllable aggregation module that selects the final top-N by maximizing a value function combining accuracy and pairwise diversity with a tunable factor λ. The diversity term can be instantiated with a simple dissimilarity (e.g., different item categories). A greedy inference algorithm approximately maximizes this objective over the K·N retrieved items, enabling explicit control of the accuracy–diversity balance; experiments show substantial diversity gains with only slight recall drops as λ increases.
paper_title: Controllable Multi-Interest Framework for Recommendation

problem: Selecting slate (k-wise) comparison queries by maximizing expected value of information (EVOI) is computationally intractable in large recommender catalogs because it requires enumerating items and solving inner maximizations for each possible user response.
method: Relax the discrete item space to continuous vectors and cast EVOI/PEU as a differentiable objective that can be optimized with gradient methods over query and recommendation slates. Using sampled utility particles U, express PEU as 1/m · 1^T (UY ◦ softmax(UX)) 1_k and jointly optimize X and Y (Alg. 2), then project any hypothetical items back to the catalog via a single deep-retrieval (DeepRetrUniq). Optionally regularize X to remain near feasible items (ContReg). This continuous Monte Carlo formulation avoids full enumeration during optimization and leverages standard ML frameworks for scalability while preserving high EVOI.
paper_title: Gradient-based Optimization for Bayesian Preference Elicitation

problem: Full-item comparisons can be cognitively demanding, and finding EVOI-optimal partial-attribute comparisons is a difficult combinatorial search over attribute subsets.
method: Define ceteris paribus semantics for partial comparisons and relax partial items to [0,1]^d. Optimize a continuous objective that computes exact EVOI at each step via deep retrieval under a logistic response model, with an L1-style regularizer that encourages each partial item to be p-hot; then project by setting the top-p attributes to 1 and the rest to 0 (ContPartial). This gradient-based approach efficiently discovers high-EVOI partial comparison queries without exhaustive subset search.
paper_title: Gradient-based Optimization for Bayesian Preference Elicitation

problem: Even with a continuous relaxation, there is a trade-off between fidelity to the discrete catalog and computational cost; practical algorithms must incorporate item structure and scale to million-item spaces.
method: Provide a taxonomy of continuous EVOI variants: ContFree (tie query and recommendation slates), ContReg (feasibility regularization), ContDeepRetr (compute PEU with deep retrieval inside the objective), and ContAlter (alternate between optimizing continuous query variables and deep-retrieving recommendation items). Use initialization strategies (Random, RandUserTopItem, Balanced) to mitigate local optima. Empirically, ContAlter/ContFree achieve competitive EVOI/regret with wall-clock times around 1–3 seconds per query even with N up to 10^6 and k up to 5, scaling better than exhaustive or greedy enumeration.
paper_title: Gradient-based Optimization for Bayesian Preference Elicitation

problem: Most conversational recommender systems in e-commerce assume users know what they want and focus on preference elicitation, overlooking the need to educate shoppers about complex product spaces. There is also no standard way to evaluate educational value alongside recommendation outcomes.
method: Introduce SalesOps, a simulation and evaluation framework for complex-product CRS that equips the Seller with a product catalog and a professional buying guide, and reveals the Shopper’s preferences gradually to mimic underspecified goals. It populates up-to-date catalogs via LLM-generated product metadata, leverages public buying guides as expert knowledge, and triggers preference revelation using semantic similarity to the seller’s questions. A multi-dimensional evaluation measures recommendation accuracy (annotated acceptable items), educational value via (i) NLI-based coverage of guide content and (ii) a knowledge quiz, and fluency/professionalism via human and GPT-4 judgments.
paper_title: Salespeople vs SalesBot: Exploring the Role of Educational Value in Conversational Recommender Systems

problem: Building a CRS that both educates and recommends requires deciding when to retrieve knowledge, when to search products, and how to ground responses under context-length limits; it is unclear which components most benefit from LLMs.
method: Develop SalesBot, a modular LLM-powered agent with: (1) an Action Decision module that selects among knowledge search, product search, or direct response; (2) Knowledge Search that generates LLM queries and retrieves relevant buying-guide passages using a FAISS retriever; (3) Product Search that embeds product metadata with SentenceTransformers to retrieve candidate items; and (4) Response Generation that conditions on retrieved knowledge or chat history, plus a regeneration step to ensure concise, complete replies. An ablation replaces LLMs or modules (e.g., smaller model for response generation, rule-based action decision, keyword query generation, no regeneration) and shows response generation with a strong LLM is most critical, LLM-based query generation improves recommendation quality over keyword queries, and regeneration boosts fluency with minor trade-offs.
paper_title: Salespeople vs SalesBot: Exploring the Role of Educational Value in Conversational Recommender Systems

problem: It is unknown how an LLM-based sales agent compares to professional salespeople on recommendation quality, educational value, and fluency, and how faithful each is to available knowledge.
method: Conduct a human study with 15 professional salespeople within SalesOps and compare to SalesBot on matched scenarios, using crowd-worker evaluation for fluency and educational value and annotated ground truth for recommendation accuracy. Analyze faithfulness by prompting GPT-4 with the buying guide, product catalog, and full conversation to detect inconsistent claims. Findings: SalesBot approaches professionals in fluency and informativeness but lags in recommendation quality; both humans and the bot produce unfaithful claims in a substantial fraction of chats, highlighting practical challenges for enforcing faithfulness in conversational sales.
paper_title: Salespeople vs SalesBot: Exploring the Role of Educational Value in Conversational Recommender Systems

problem: In graph contrastive learning (CL) for recommendation, performance gains are widely attributed to graph structure augmentations (e.g., node/edge dropout), yet it remains unclear whether these augmentations are actually necessary and what mechanism drives the improvements. Additionally, graph-based recommenders suffer from popularity bias and representation collapse toward hubs.
method: The paper conducts an empirical analysis of SGL variants, including a no-augmentation version (SGL-WA), and visualizes/quantifies embedding uniformity to explain CL’s effect. Results show SGL-WA performs comparably to or better than several augmentation variants (e.g., SGL-ND/RW), indicating the InfoNCE loss—not graph augmentation—is the decisive factor. By rewriting the loss, the authors argue InfoNCE pushes embeddings toward uniformity on the unit hypersphere, implicitly mitigating popularity bias; this is corroborated via KDE/t-SNE plots and a uniformity metric that aligns with performance trends.
paper_title: Are Graph Augmentations Necessary? Simple Graph Contrastive Learning for Recommendation

problem: Dropout-based graph augmentations are fragile (risking semantic distortion by dropping critical nodes/edges) and inefficient (requiring adjacency reconstruction every epoch), motivating an augmentation approach that is both more effective and more efficient while enabling fine control over representation uniformity.
method: SimGCL replaces graph augmentations with representation-level perturbations: at each LightGCN layer, it adds small, directed random noise to node embeddings to create two contrastive views, with constraints Δ = ̄Δ ⊙ sign(e) and ||Δ||2 = ε to keep perturbed views informative yet close. The model optimizes a joint BPR + InfoNCE objective, where ε smoothly regulates uniformity and thus debiasing strength. This simple, augmentation-free design avoids adjacency reconstruction, accelerates convergence, and improves accuracy; experiments on three benchmarks show consistent gains over SGL variants, faster training (fewer epochs, lower per-epoch overhead than edge-dropout SGL), and stronger long-tail recommendation performance.
paper_title: Are Graph Augmentations Necessary? Simple Graph Contrastive Learning for Recommendation

problem: Learning policies offline for many similar contextual bandit tasks (e.g., multi-user recommendation) is data-inefficient if each task is solved independently, and off-policy methods must be pessimistic about actions not well covered by logged data.
method: Introduce HierOPO, a hierarchical off-policy optimization algorithm that builds pessimistic lower-confidence-bound (LCB) reward estimates by decomposing the posterior mean and covariance of each task’s parameters via the law of total expectation and covariance. It conditions on a shared hyper-parameter to capture cross-task structure, separates uncertainty from the hyper-parameter and the task data, and acts by maximizing LCBs. In linear-Gaussian models, HierOPO yields closed-form, efficiently computable LCBs using per-task sufficient statistics (G_s, B_s) and a Gaussian hyper-posterior, producing tighter estimates than flat (non-hierarchical) baselines and not requiring knowledge of the logging policy.
paper_title: Multi-Task Off-Policy Learning from Bandit Feedback

problem: There is a lack of theory quantifying how hierarchical structure improves sample efficiency in off-policy multi-task bandits, especially with guarantees that reflect both within-task data and across-task sharing.
method: Derive Bayesian per-task suboptimality bounds for HierOPO in linear-Gaussian models that split into a task term (analogous to single-task linear bandits) and a hyper-parameter term capturing uncertainty in the shared prior. The task term decays as O(1/√n_s) and benefits from informative task priors, while the hyper-parameter term shrinks with more tasks and vanishes as the number of tasks grows, approaching the oracle that knows the hyper-parameter. The bounds strictly improve over flat off-policy methods that ignore hierarchy (which effectively inflate uncertainty), and constitute, to the authors’ knowledge, the first Bayesian bounds for off-policy optimization.
paper_title: Multi-Task Off-Policy Learning from Bandit Feedback

problem: Practical validation is needed to show that hierarchical off-policy learning yields better policies under limited logged data in real applications like recommendation.
method: Evaluate HierOPO against OracleOPO and FlatOPO on synthetic multi-task bandits and a MovieLens multi-user recommendation task. For MovieLens, construct user/movie latent factors via matrix factorization and estimate the hyper-prior by clustering users with a Gaussian mixture model, then learn from uniformly randomized logged data. HierOPO consistently outperforms FlatOPO and is close to OracleOPO, with the largest gains in high hyper-parameter uncertainty and low-data regimes.
paper_title: Multi-Task Off-Policy Learning from Bandit Feedback

problem: Two-stage query-focused meeting summarization depends on extracting query-relevant utterances from long transcripts, but prior extractors either use hard binary labels or ROUGE-score regression, which poorly capture the relative ordering of utterances and lead to suboptimal generator inputs.
method: Introduce a Ranker-Generator pipeline that learns query relevance via pairwise ranking instead of classification/regression. Gold relevance orders are derived by sorting utterances by ROUGE(U, S*) with the gold summary; a cross-encoder (MiniLM) predicts relevance scores and is trained with a margin-based pairwise loss to prefer higher-ranked utterances. Utterances are partitioned into samples to control pairwise complexity, and the top-K utterances after ranking are concatenated and fed to a BART generator. This ranking-based objective better aligns with the extractor’s goal of producing correct relative orders, improving query relevance and overall summarization quality.
paper_title: Learning to Rank Utterances for Query-Focused Meeting Summarization

problem: Pairwise ranking within samples is efficient but yields inconsistent ordering across samples, and full pairwise ranking over all utterances is computationally prohibitive; thus, obtaining an accurate global top-K order is challenging.
method: Add a global listwise re-ranking module that refines the cross-sample order of candidates by optimizing a top-k permutation probability distribution, inspired by ListNet. Specifically, it minimizes the KL divergence between predicted and gold top1–topk distributions over the gathered top utterances from all samples, focusing the loss on the highest ranks that matter for the generator. This two-stage design—sample-level pairwise filtering followed by listwise top-k re-ranking—yields more precise top-K inputs; ablations show removing re-ranking drops performance by roughly 2.5 ROUGE points across R-1/2/L.
paper_title: Learning to Rank Utterances for Query-Focused Meeting Summarization

problem: Zero-shot LLMs often struggle to make accurate, personalized rating predictions because preferences are subjective and dispersed across rich user histories and item metadata.
method: Use zero-shot chain-of-thought prompting that presents the entire interaction context in natural language—preamble, sequential user history with reviews and ratings, and new item metadata—and explicitly asks the model to reason before outputting a 1–5 rating. This CoT setup yields consistent gains over direct prediction and naive baselines, and ablations show that removing user reviews (and ratings) severely degrades performance, highlighting the importance of rich textual feedback for eliciting useful reasoning.
paper_title: Leveraging LLM Reasoning Enhances Personalized Recommender Systems

problem: Running large LLMs with CoT at inference is expensive; smaller models often lack reasoning ability for personalized recommendation tasks.
method: Distill reasoning from a stronger LLM by sampling multiple diverse reasoning–rating pairs per user–item at different decoding temperatures, then fine-tune smaller sequence-to-sequence models (e.g., Flan-T5) to generate reasoning followed by the rating using these sampled reasons plus gold rating labels as targets. Optional filters compare the large model’s predicted ratings to ground truth (5-class, binary, 1-off). Fine-tuning with reasoning consistently outperforms rating-only training, scales with model size, benefits from multiple reasoning paths, and shows domain-dependent effects of filtering.
paper_title: Leveraging LLM Reasoning Enhances Personalized Recommender Systems

problem: Vector-quantization IVF indexes for dense retrieval are lossy: at low latency they often miss relevant documents unless many clusters are probed, which sharply increases runtime.
method: Hybrid Inverted Index (HI2) organizes two complementary inverted lists per corpus: embedding clusters (semantic) and salient terms (lexical). Each document is indexed to its nearest cluster (via KMeans centroids) and to K_T1 top-scored terms (BM25 or a BERT+MLP scorer). At query time, HI2 dispatches to the top-K_C clusters (by query–centroid similarity) and to K_T2 terms (all tokens if short, otherwise top by precomputed average term score), merges candidates, and evaluates them with PQ/OPQ. This hybrid candidate generation leverages the complementarity of clusters and terms to achieve higher recall at lower latency than IVF and even HNSW while approaching brute-force quality.
paper_title: Hybrid Inverted Index Is a Robust Accelerator for Dense Retrieval

problem: Unsupervised cluster/term selection (KMeans, BM25) is strong but not optimal; we need a principled way to learn the hybrid index selectors directly for retrieval effectiveness without sacrificing efficiency.
method: HI2_sup introduces a joint knowledge distillation objective to train cluster embeddings and the term-scoring network end-to-end against a teacher built from off-the-shelf dense embeddings. It minimizes KL divergence between the teacher’s softmaxed relevance over a sampled document set and (a) the cluster selector’s distribution derived from query–centroid similarities mapped to documents’ assigned clusters, and (b) the term selector’s distribution from query/document term-score vector similarities; a commitment loss keeps document embeddings close to their assigned cluster. This learning substantially boosts recall and approaches the teacher/brute-force effectiveness at low latency.
paper_title: Hybrid Inverted Index Is a Robust Accelerator for Dense Retrieval

problem: There is no scalable, objective way to evaluate the quality of subjective, personalized reasoning in recommender systems without curated gold explanations or human raters.
method: Introduce Rec-SAVER, an automatic reasoning evaluation pipeline that (1) generates post hoc explanations conditioned on user history, item metadata, and the ground-truth rating, (2) removes rating-leaking phrases, and (3) self-verifies each explanation by asking the LLM to predict the rating from it, retaining only explanations that reproduce the gold label as verified references. Reasoning outputs from target models are then scored against this verified pool with BLEU/ROUGE/METEOR/BERTScore. Human studies show these metrics correlate with coherence and faithfulness, and the self-verification step improves correlations, enabling reference-free, scalable reasoning assessment.
paper_title: Leveraging LLM Reasoning Enhances Personalized Recommender Systems

problem: Selecting salient query terms can add undesirable online cost if it relies on heavy neural processing, yet accurate lexical cues are needed to recover relevant documents missed by clustering.
method: HI2 makes query-time term selection lightweight by performing all heavy term scoring offline on documents (BM25 or BERT+MLP), storing per-term scores and the global average score per term. At search time, it selects all tokens for short queries and only the top-K_T2 tokens by average score for long queries, avoiding neural computation on queries. This keeps query dispatch overhead negligible while preserving the lexical benefits that improve candidate recall and overall efficiency.
paper_title: Hybrid Inverted Index Is a Robust Accelerator for Dense Retrieval

problem: Distance-based recommenders can capture fine-grained preferences via triangle-inequality-respecting metrics, but prior methods use deterministic embeddings and ignore uncertainty in learned user/item representations.
method: Represent each user and item as a diagonal-covariance Gaussian and measure preference using the squared 2-Wasserstein distance between the user and item distributions. This metric both respects the triangle inequality and explicitly incorporates uncertainty via means and covariances, with a closed-form expression for Gaussians and linear time in embedding dimension (W2 for Gaussian pairs).
paper_title: Probabilistic Metric Learning with Adaptive Margin for Top-K Recommendation

problem: Standard margin ranking loss uses a fixed margin across all triplets, which is suboptimal and requires heavy tuning; directly learning margins can collapse them toward zero, harming training.
method: Generate a per-triplet adaptive margin with a neural network f(i,j,k;Φ) trained via a bilevel optimization scheme: an inner objective learns model parameters Θ using the adaptive-margin loss, and an outer objective guides Φ using a fixed-margin loss through a one-step gradient-based proxy. The network takes a designed feature s_ijk built from sampled user/item embeddings with element-wise distance indicators and uses softplus to ensure positive margins; reparameterization enables backprop through Gaussian sampling. Alternating updates of Θ and Φ avoid trivial small margins and yield better ranking performance.
paper_title: Probabilistic Metric Learning with Adaptive Margin for Top-K Recommendation

problem: Existing metric-learning recommenders often neglect explicit user-user and item-item relations, leading to embeddings that fail to cluster similar users or items.
method: Compute user-user and item-item similarities from the implicit feedback matrix (e.g., cosine similarity with thresholds to define neighbors), and add two margin ranking losses with adaptive margins to explicitly pull similar users/items closer than random ones. These auxiliary losses share the adaptive-margin generation mechanism and are combined with the main user-item objective, producing substantial gains shown in ablations and overall results.
paper_title: Probabilistic Metric Learning with Adaptive Margin for Top-K Recommendation

problem: Federated recommender systems must protect user privacy and run under tight on-device resource constraints; prior federated methods typically fine-tune a shared large model per device, which burdens storage/communication and weakly exploits collaborative filtering (CF) across users.
method: MetaMF places a server-side meta network that generates per-user private models instead of copying/fine-tuning a shared one. A collaborative memory (CM) maps a user’s one-hot id to a user embedding and treats it as coordinates into a shared memory matrix to produce a collaborative vector that fuses CF information across all users. A meta recommender (MR) then consumes this vector to generate both the user-specific MLP rating predictor and the user-specific item embedding matrix; only this small private predictor and embeddings are deployed on the device. Training is federated: devices compute gradients for their private predictor on local data and upload them; the server updates the CM/MR meta network and regenerates per-user parameters. This architecture keeps on-device models small while leveraging CF on the server, achieving competitive RP accuracy with fewer local parameters.
paper_title: Meta Matrix Factorization for Federated Rating Predictions

problem: Directly generating per-user high-dimensional embeddings for many items is computationally and memory intensive in a federated setting.
method: MetaMF introduces a rise-dimensional generation (RG) strategy: conditioned on the user’s collaborative vector, it first generates a low-dimensional item embedding matrix and a rise-dimensional matrix via MLPs, reshapes them, and multiplies them to obtain the high-dimensional item embeddings. This reduces the parameter/compute requirement from O(di×n) to O(s×n + di×s) with s ≪ di, making per-user item embeddings practical at scale.
paper_title: Meta Matrix Factorization for Federated Rating Predictions

problem: Do modality-based recommender models (that encode item content such as text or images) truly rival or surpass traditional ID-based models in regular and warm-start item recommendation, beyond cold-start cases? Prior work lacked fair, apples-to-apples comparisons under identical backbones and settings.
method: Perform a rigorous head-to-head study by replacing itemID embeddings with strong pre-trained modality encoders (BERT/RoBERTa for text; ResNet/Swin for images) and training end-to-end within identical recommender backbones (two-tower DSSM vs sequential SASRec) using the same loss, sampling, and hyperparameter protocol, with a DT-layer for dimension matching. Across three large-scale datasets (MIND text; HM and Bili images), results show that with the SASRec (seq2seq) backbone, MoRec matches or outperforms IDRec for text and is on par for vision even in warm-start settings, whereas with DSSM MoRec lags behind IDRec (e.g., Table 2, Table 3; lines 153–161, 169–180).
paper_title: Where to Go Next for Recommender Systems? ID- vs. Modality-based Recommender Models Revisited

problem: Can recommender systems inherit advances from NLP and CV—i.e., do larger/stronger and pre-trained modality encoders translate into higher recommendation accuracy?
method: Systematically vary modality encoders by size and strength and compare pre-trained vs training-from-scratch: BERT tiny/small/base, RoBERTa base, OPT, TextCNN, GloVe for text; ResNet18/34/50 and Swin-T/B for vision. Empirically, larger and stronger encoders generally yield higher HR/NDCG, and pre-trained encoders beat training-from-scratch, especially on smaller datasets and for vision (Figure 4; Table 4; lines 186–209). This establishes that MoRec can inherit progress from NLP/CV into RS.
paper_title: Where to Go Next for Recommender Systems? ID- vs. Modality-based Recommender Models Revisited

problem: Are modality representations from foundation models universal enough to be used as frozen features in RS, or is task-specific adaptation via end-to-end training necessary? What are the practical implications for efficiency and stability?
method: Compare two-stage pipelines (pre-extract frozen modality features fed to the recommender, with/without additional MLP layers) against end-to-end fine-tuning of the modality encoder jointly with the RS backbone. Two-stage underperforms markedly—especially for image recommendation—while E2E delivers substantially better accuracy (Table 5; lines 215–221). The study also surfaces key practical challenges for E2E MoRec: far higher training cost than IDRec (often >100× compute/time; Table 6; lines 227–258) and training instability (model collapse) without carefully set learning rates for modality vs other modules (Figure 5; lines 277–299).
paper_title: Where to Go Next for Recommender Systems? ID- vs. Modality-based Recommender Models Revisited

problem: CTR models often learn feature interactions implicitly and rarely tailor representations to the different structures of dense (numerical) and sparse (categorical) features. In particular, dense feature crossings are typically ignored or handled suboptimally, limiting expressiveness and interpretability.
method: XCrossNet introduces a feature-structure-oriented architecture with explicit interaction modeling per structure. For dense features, it uses stacked cross layers whose outputs approximate multivariate polynomials of increasing degree, enabling explicit high-order crossings (lines 69–95). For sparse features, it embeds one-hot fields and applies a product layer that forms vector-valued interactions by concatenating weighted sums of pairwise inner products; the weights are factorized as W^2,t_ij = Θ^t_i · Θ^t_j to reduce parameters (lines 105–120, 111–114). The model then concatenates dense and sparse cross features and applies an additional cross layer to capture cross-structure interactions (lines 121–129), followed by an MLP for non-linear selection and importance weighting (lines 129–136). This explicit, efficient design yields improved performance over strong baselines (lines 161–181, 198).
paper_title: XCrossNet: Feature Structure-Oriented Learning for Click-Through Rate Prediction

problem: When combining dense and sparse features, a dimensionality imbalance can cause sparse features to dominate, degrading CTR prediction quality.
method: XCrossNet mitigates feature dimensionality imbalance by controlling the dimensionality of cross dense features via the depth of its dense cross layers, with dim(OC) = M · l (lines 208–211). It defines a balance index dim(OC) dim(OP) / M N (line 210) and empirically shows that increasing the dense cross depth stabilizes and improves AUC by better balancing dense and sparse contributions (lines 208–211).
paper_title: XCrossNet: Feature Structure-Oriented Learning for Click-Through Rate Prediction

problem: Single user-embedding approaches collapse a user’s multiple, evolving interests into one vector, limiting relevance and diversity; prior multi-embedding methods often fix the number of embeddings or are hard to serve at scale.
method: PinnerSage clusters each user’s recent action pins in a fixed multimodal item-embedding space (PinSage integrates visual, text, and graph signals) using Ward hierarchical clustering, which automatically determines the number of concept-coherent clusters. Each cluster is represented by a medoid pin (not a centroid) to avoid topic drift, improve robustness to outliers, and enable lightweight storage/caching via pin IDs. A time-decayed importance score per cluster guides sampling of up to three medoids per request; their embeddings query an ANN index to retrieve candidates. This removes constraints on the number of per-user embeddings and couples medoid sampling with decayed importance to track recency and frequency.
paper_title: PinnerSage: Multi-Modal User Embedding Framework for Recommendations at Pinterest

problem: Nearest-neighbor candidate retrieval over billions of items must be accurate and cost/latency-efficient; naive indexing returns near-duplicates and is expensive to serve.
method: The system adopts HNSW for ANN indexing of pin embeddings, which outperforms LSH Orthoplex on cost/latency/recall. It refines the candidate pool by filtering near-duplicates and low-quality pins, and caches queries at the medoid pin-ID level (rather than raw embedding vectors). Together these choices significantly cut serving cost (e.g., ~60% vs LSH, ~50% via index refinement, ~75% from medoid caching) while maintaining high-recall retrieval.
paper_title: PinnerSage: Multi-Modal User Embedding Framework for Recommendations at Pinterest

problem: User interests shift in real time, but recomputing full multi-embedding profiles continuously at web scale is infeasible; systems must balance long-term preferences with fast adaptation.
method: PinnerSage employs a two-pronged serving architecture: a daily batch MapReduce job clusters the last 90 days of actions to produce medoids and time-decayed importance scores stored in a key-value store, while a lightweight online component incrementally updates clusters using the user’s most recent actions streamed in real time (e.g., last ~20). The batch and online outputs are combined, and updated importance scores steer medoid sampling for retrieval, yielding both responsiveness and long-term stability.
paper_title: PinnerSage: Multi-Modal User Embedding Framework for Recommendations at Pinterest

problem: Cloze-style pre-training with masked language models is inefficient (only a subset of tokens are trained per pass, and n passes are needed to score a sequence) and relies on a softmax over the full vocabulary, creating a pre-train/fine-tune mismatch and expensive likelihood computations.
method: Introduce Electric, an energy-based cloze model that assigns a scalar energy to each input token in context, avoiding masking and an output softmax. Train it with conditional noise-contrastive estimation (NCE), and make training efficient by replacing k≈15% tokens at once and computing the NCE loss in a single forward pass (Algorithm 2), relying on a small discrepancy between original and noised contexts. Use a lightweight two-tower cloze model (left-to-right and right-to-left transformers) as the learned noise distribution q trained jointly. The NCE objective encourages self-normalization (Zθ≈1), enabling unnormalized token probabilities without computing a partition function and yielding better downstream transfer than BERT.
paper_title: Pre-Training Transformers as Energy-Based Cloze Models

problem: Pseudo-log-likelihood (PLL) scoring for reranking text outputs (e.g., ASR/NMT n-best lists) is slow with masked LMs (n forward passes per sequence) and left-to-right LMs can underperform for reranking compared to bidirectional models.
method: Leverage Electric’s bidirectional, energy-based token scoring to compute PLLs for all tokens in a single transformer pass. Apply these PLLs to rerank 100-best ASR hypotheses on LibriSpeech via linear interpolation with the ASR score. Electric achieves lower WER than GPT-2 trained on comparable data and runs in 1 pass versus n for BERT, while being competitive with or better than ELECTRA-TT, demonstrating fast and effective reranking.
paper_title: Pre-Training Transformers as Energy-Based Cloze Models

problem: ELECTRA’s discriminator training objective lacks a clear probabilistic interpretation in terms of cloze modeling, making it unclear what distribution is being learned and how it relates to token likelihoods.
method: Formulate a principled connection between ELECTRA and energy-based cloze modeling: Electric’s NCE-based discriminator directly models pdata(xt|x\t) by incorporating the noise distribution q into the decision rule, whereas ELECTRA’s discriminator uses a sigmoid over energies and effectively performs negative sampling between pdata and q. This framing explains that ELECTRA learns to distinguish data from generator samples, while Electric learns pdata and provides direct (unnormalized) token probabilities, clarifying the trade-offs (e.g., Electric requires a two-tower q but yields useful token likelihoods).
paper_title: Pre-Training Transformers as Energy-Based Cloze Models

problem: Visually guided binaural audio generation typically requires paired video and binaural recordings, which are scarce and lead to poor generalization when trained under fully supervised settings.
method: Introduce PseudoBinaural, a binaural-recording-free pipeline that constructs pseudo visual-stereo pairs from mono videos. It (1) maps mono to binaural via a Mono-Binaural-Mapping that decomposes the source using zero- and first-order spherical harmonics (ambisonics W, X, Y, Z) and renders binaural signals by decoding through HRIR with a virtual speaker array to account for room reverberations; and (2) aligns visuals to spatial directions via a Visual-Coordinate-Mapping from image pixels to spherical angles, then mixes multiple mono sources with position- and depth-aware scaling to synthesize scenes. A U-Net audio backbone with a visual encoder is trained to predict the left-right STFT difference, from which binaural channels are reconstructed.
paper_title: Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios

problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
method: Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.
paper_title: Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios

problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
method: Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.
paper_title: Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios

problem: Bahasa lacks a high-quality, well-curated TTS corpus with sufficient scale and linguistic diversity to train modern neural TTS systems.
method: The authors construct the Bahasa Harmony dataset: ~55 hours and 52K recordings from two proficient native speakers (male/female), recorded in a controlled studio setup, with text sourced from Wikipedia and ChatGPT translations to increase linguistic variety. The corpus includes balanced train/validation/test splits and extensive statistics (e.g., 458K words, 23K vocabulary), providing a strong foundation for training and evaluation of Bahasa TTS models.
paper_title: Bahasa Harmony: A Comprehensive Dataset for Bahasa Text-to-Speech Synthesis with Discrete Codec Modeling of EnGen-TTS.

problem: Building a TTS system that produces natural Bahasa speech while being efficient and robust across speakers using discrete neural codec modeling.
method: They propose EnGen-TTS, which freezes a multilingual T5 (mT5) encoder for text conditioning and a pretrained EnCodec audio tokenizer; only the first RVQ code stream is used to represent phonetic/content information, reducing sequence complexity. A neural codec language model autoregressively predicts audio tokens via masked self-attention and dual cross-attention: first to text embeddings, then to speaker embeddings from a frozen speaker encoder, improving generalization versus prior concatenation approaches. Training uses a composite objective combining cross-entropy on audio tokens, mel-spectrogram loss, and an adversarial GAN loss (with tuned weights), and a HiFi-GAN vocoder for waveform synthesis; inference efficiency is improved with KV-cache. This design achieves state-of-the-art MOS on the Bahasa set (4.45 ± 0.13) with a low real-time factor (~0.016).
paper_title: Bahasa Harmony: A Comprehensive Dataset for Bahasa Text-to-Speech Synthesis with Discrete Codec Modeling of EnGen-TTS.

problem: Achieving strong multilingual TTS performance, especially for low-resource languages, with a model that transfers well across languages.
method: The system leverages transfer learning: models are first trained on LJSpeech and VCTK to learn text–audio alignments, then fine-tuned on target language data. The mT5-based text conditioning and codec LM enable effective cross-lingual synthesis. Evaluations across seven languages (Spanish, Portuguese, German, Dutch, Hindi, Marathi, Tamil) show consistent MOS improvements over VITS and NaturalSpeech, including large gains in low-resource Indic languages, demonstrating robustness and adaptability of the approach.
paper_title: Bahasa Harmony: A Comprehensive Dataset for Bahasa Text-to-Speech Synthesis with Discrete Codec Modeling of EnGen-TTS.

problem: Contextual ASR performance degrades on rare and out-of-vocabulary words, but automatically discovering the right context without modifying/fine-tuning the ASR model or relying on structured catalogs remains challenging.
method: Introduce CB-RAG: a model-agnostic, embedding-based retrieval method that builds a query from the concatenated transcripts of the previous k VAD-detected segments and encodes it with a MiniLM sentence transformer (all-MiniLM-L6-v2). It retrieves Top-N nearest words via cosine similarity in a FAISS index over a large, frozen vocabulary of 466k non-stopword entries (augmented with definitions when available), and passes these as context to a black-box contextual biasing ASR. Across TED-LIUMv3, Earnings21, and SPGISpeech, CB-RAG reduces WER by up to ~17% relative to no-context while keeping latency close to baseline (≈1.02–1.36×).
paper_title: Retrieval Augmented Generation based context discovery for ASR

problem: In black-box, plug-and-play settings, it is unclear whether LLMs should be used to generate context or to post-correct transcripts, and what accuracy–latency trade-offs these strategies entail compared to retrieval-based context.
method: Benchmark two LLM-based plug-and-play alternatives within the same pipeline: (1) CB-LLM, which prompts an LLM with the prior transcript window to generate a list of relevant words that are deduplicated and stopword-filtered; and (2) LLM-fix, which post-edits ASR outputs conditioned on history and context via an instruction prompt. Results show LLM-based methods achieve higher contextual overlap but are 4.66–6.16× slower and do not surpass CB-RAG in WER; LLM-fix yields only marginal gains (e.g., 16.9%→16.8% WER on TED-LIUMv3).
paper_title: Retrieval Augmented Generation based context discovery for ASR

problem: There is a need for a standardized, deployable pipeline to fairly compare context extraction and correction methods for long-form ASR without access to model internals and under realistic latency constraints.
method: Provide an end-to-end, model-agnostic pipeline: VAD-based segmentation; per-segment context construction from the preceding k segments; contextual ASR decoding; optional post-ASR LLM correction; and consistent evaluation using WER, contextual overlap, context size, and runtime. Include oracle and no-context baselines and apply the pipeline to TED-LIUMv3, Earnings21, and SPGISpeech with unified preprocessing for fair comparison.
paper_title: Retrieval Augmented Generation based context discovery for ASR

problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
method: Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).
paper_title: RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings

problem: There is a lack of publicly available, reproducible AED datasets with ASR outputs, word-level confidences, and error annotations; reliance on paid cloud ASR systems also hampers comparability as models evolve.
method: Create and release a novel AED dataset by transcribing the LibriSpeech corpus with Google Cloud Speech-to-Text (default and video models), recording hypotheses and word-level confidences, and aligning hypotheses to references via minimum edit distance to label each word as ERROR/NOTERROR. The dataset includes both clean and other pools with detailed statistics and supports reproducible training/evaluation independent of cloud ASR availability or changes.
paper_title: RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings

problem: In federated learning, some clients may hold irrelevant or corrupted data whose updates destabilize or degrade the central model. The server must identify and prioritize relevant clients without accessing their private data, based only on shared updates.
method: Model client updates in each communication round as players in a cooperative game where the characteristic function is the model’s validation performance when only a subset’s updates are applied. Compute per-client Shapley values (possibly negative) via permutation-based Monte Carlo to quantify each client’s marginal contribution, and maintain a server-side relevance vector ϕ updated as ϕ_k ← α·ϕ_k + β·sv[k]. Sample clients in subsequent rounds according to Pϕ = softmax(ϕ), yielding S-FedAvg that prunes irrelevant clients and stabilizes training. With m=5 selected clients per round, the Shapley computation is tractable and is approximated with R=10 permutations in experiments, leading to smoother convergence vs. FedAvg.
paper_title: Game of Gradients: Mitigating Irrelevant Clients in Federated Learning

problem: Servers often need to detect which clients have the best data for a specific target class to address class imbalance or to incentivize class-specific contributions in federated settings.
method: Extend S-FedAvg to compute class-specific relevance by restricting the validation set to examples of a target class c (V_c) when evaluating coalitions in the Shapley computation. This yields per-class relevance scores ϕ^c that elevate clients whose updates most improve accuracy on class c, enabling the server to rank/select class-specific best clients. Empirically, clients owning data of the queried class achieve consistently higher ϕ^c than others.
paper_title: Game of Gradients: Mitigating Irrelevant Clients in Federated Learning

problem: Face-to-speech systems struggle to generate speech that simultaneously matches a person’s identity and emotional expression from only a face (no speech prompt) while staying aligned with the text content.
method: DEmoFace introduces an RVQ-based discrete diffusion framework that synthesizes speech tokens from visual and text cues. Speech is discretized with a 12-layer RVQ codec (1024-size codebooks), and a Multimodal DiT (MM-DiT) performs reverse denoising with 12 output heads to predict concrete scores per RVQ level. Global face-style conditioning is injected via AdaLN using separate identity and emotion embeddings (identity from ArcFace+FaceNet aligned to GE2E with cosine/L1/L2 losses; emotion from POSTER V2 labels with a learnable, identity-agnostic embedding), and text is aligned via cross-attention over SpeechT5 features with rotary positions.
paper_title: Emotional Face-to-Speech

problem: Training on all RVQ token levels at once is inefficient and harms quality due to spectral bias; models need to learn low-frequency structure before high-frequency detail.
method: A coarse-to-fine curriculum learning scheme progressively introduces RVQ levels: the model starts with low-level (low-frequency) tokens and adds higher-level tokens every 3 epochs, using a multi-level DSE loss across current levels. This leverages the RVQ frequency hierarchy to improve efficiency and generation quality, as validated by ablations on EmoSim/SpkSim, MCD, and WER.
paper_title: Emotional Face-to-Speech

problem: Client labels may be permuted/inconsistent with the server’s label convention, causing otherwise relevant clients to appear harmful and reducing overall performance.
method: Introduce S-FedAvg-Label-Std: run S-FedAvg until the validation performance stabilizes, then the server signals potentially corrupted clients and shares a per-class precision dictionary P_dict. Each client partitions its local data by its current labels, predicts with the current global model, and for each partition swaps its local label to the majority predicted label L if the majority fraction exceeds P_dict(L). After clients correct labels, the server resets their relevance to mean(ϕ) to reduce prior penalties, increasing their sampling probability. This procedure improves test accuracy over both FedAvg and S-FedAvg.
paper_title: Game of Gradients: Mitigating Irrelevant Clients in Federated Learning

problem: Standard predictor-free guidance is not well-suited to multi-conditional generation (identity, emotion, text) in discrete diffusion, leading to entangled or weak control.
method: Enhanced predictor-free guidance (EPFG) reframes guidance via an energy-based view to combine joint and compositional conditioning. It multiplies a joint-guided score with per-condition guided scores, controlled by a joint scale w0 and per-condition scales wk, and is applied during Euler sampling. EPFG improves multi-conditional control and quality, with ablations showing drops in EmoSim/SpkSim and WER when EPFG is removed.
paper_title: Emotional Face-to-Speech

problem: Robots need reliable, early slip detection during grasp-and-lift across diverse objects; prior GelSight-only, threshold-based methods struggle, especially on smooth or low-texture surfaces and when gel stretch mimics motion.
method: Fuse tactile and visual sensing with a CNN-LSTM that ingests paired 8-frame sequences from a GelSight fingertip sensor and an external gripper-mounted camera. Per-frame features are extracted with a fixed pretrained CNN (VGG-16/19 or Inception-V3), features from the two sensors are concatenated and reduced via a fully connected layer, then two LSTM layers (64 units) model temporal dynamics before a final classifier with dropout regularization. Trained on 84 objects (1102 grasps; augmented to 5510 sequences via sliding window) and tested on 10 unseen objects (152 grasps), the best model (Inception-V3, raw images, tactile+vision) achieves 88.03% accuracy, outperforming tactile-only/vision-only variants and a prior GelSight thresholding approach (≈71%), and detecting slip earlier with shorter sequences than the baseline.
paper_title: Slip Detection with Combined Tactile and Visual Information

problem: With limited data and ImageNet-pretrained CNNs, vision-only slip detection fails because these features suppress absolute position and motion cues critical for slip.
method: Introduce an image-difference input that subtracts each frame from a pre-lift reference (T(-2)) and adds an offset (128), explicitly highlighting motion for the pretrained backbone. This representation substantially boosts vision-only performance from ~53–55% with raw frames to ~77–81% across VGG-16/19 and Inception-V3, and is also competitive in the fused tactile-vision models.
paper_title: Slip Detection with Combined Tactile and Visual Information

problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
method: Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.
paper_title: Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation

problem: On-policy adaptation on real robots is sample-inefficient and risky in high-dimensional, low-level action spaces, leading to unsafe or erratic exploration.
method: Train a variational autoencoder on a dataset of safe, task-specific robot trajectories to define a low-dimensional latent action space; the policy outputs latent vectors conditioned on the goal, which are decoded into smooth executable trajectories. This reduces action dimensionality, focuses learning on terminal rewards, and enables faster, safer on-policy adaptation. The learned 2D latent space disentangles control factors (e.g., hitting angle vs. strength), yielding predictable control and stable updates.
paper_title: Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation

problem: Deep RL in sparse-reward robotic tasks struggles due to poor exploration; human evaluative feedback could help but EEG-based error-related potentials (ErrPs) are noisy and human labeling time is limited.
method: Brain-Guided RL: a three-stage approach. (1) Calibrate an EEG ErrP classifier using a modified EEGNet (valid padding; reduced filters) to detect observation errors from scalp EEG with typical accuracies of 55–75%. (2) During online observation, learn a Human Feedback function F and policy π_HF via supervised learning from noisy brain-derived labels, using a low-dimensional state/action design, a lightweight FC network (1 hidden layer, 16 units), and a prioritized feedback replay buffer to reuse scarce labels. (3) Train an RL policy (PPO) from sparse rewards while blending behavior with π_HF via a linearly decaying ε_HF (e.g., ε_HF,init=0.8 decaying to 0), so π_HF guides early exploration and RL eventually takes over; unlike prior work, feedback is not used directly as reward, reducing human involvement and improving robustness to EEG noise.
paper_title: Accelerated Robot Learning via Human Brain Signals

problem: It is unclear how effective and robust EEG-driven guidance is across different feedback accuracies, and whether it can practically accelerate learning on embodied navigation tasks.
method: Conduct extensive simulation with a noisy oracle at 55%, 60%, and 70% feedback accuracy on Gibson-based mobile navigation (SSSG, VSSG), then validate with real EEG from 7 subjects. Results show that with ≥60% accuracy, π_HF + sparse RL matches rich-reward RL and substantially outperforms sparse-only RL, which fails to learn; real-user studies show 5/7 subjects (ErrP accuracy 0.60–0.67) successfully guide RL to superior asymptotic performance, while two lower-accuracy cases (0.56–0.57) fail—demonstrating both effectiveness and the accuracy threshold for practical gains.
paper_title: Accelerated Robot Learning via Human Brain Signals

problem: 6-DoF grasp detection in cluttered scenes wastes computation and reduces accuracy by uniformly sampling candidate grasp points and ignoring where to grasp.
method: Define a geometry-driven quality, graspness, that scores point locations and approach directions by a look-ahead search. For each point and sampled view, generate grasp candidates via grid sampling over in-plane rotations and approach depths, score them with an analytic force model (Dex-Net) and filter by a threshold; extend to scenes by reconstructing the scene and performing collision checking before projecting and normalizing scores to obtain point-wise and view-wise scene-level graspness. This produces an object-agnostic ‘graspable landscape’ that concentrates sampling on highly graspable regions.
paper_title: Graspness Discovery in Clutters for Fast and Accurate Grasp Detection

problem: Direct look-ahead search to compute graspness is too slow for practical use; a fast, general module is needed to predict graspable regions and views in single-view point clouds.
method: Propose a Cascaded Graspness Model (CGM): a sparse-convolution ResUNet14 backbone extracts point features; an MLP predicts point-wise graspness and objectness to select seed points via thresholding and farthest-point sampling; a second MLP predicts view-wise graspness for each seed. During training, probabilistic view selection samples views proportional to predicted graspness. CGM is transferable across objects, viewpoints, and sensors, and serves as a plug-and-play sampler that boosts accuracy and speed of existing methods (e.g., GPD, PointNetGPD, and GraspNet baselines) while running at ~0.1s per scene.
paper_title: Graspness Discovery in Clutters for Fast and Accurate Grasp Detection

problem: End-to-end 6-DoF grasp detection needs to be both accurate and efficient by focusing computation on promising regions and views.
method: Introduce GSNet, a two-stage network that integrates CGM for early filtering and a Grasp Operation Model (GOM) for grasp generation. GSNet groups points in directional cylinder spaces around each selected seed point–view pair, aggregates features with a shared PointNet, and predicts grasp scores and gripper widths over discretized in-plane rotations and approach depths, selecting the best-scoring combination. Trained with a multi-task loss over objectness, point/view graspness, scores, and widths, GSNet achieves large AP gains (30+ AP over prior art on GraspNet-1Billion) while maintaining high inference speed.
paper_title: Graspness Discovery in Clutters for Fast and Accurate Grasp Detection

problem: End-to-end visuomotor policies are highly sensitive to the choice of action space (joints vs. Cartesian), and explicit policies that try to use multiple spaces face conflicting gradients and loss-balancing issues, making it hard for a single model to exploit the strengths of each space across diverse manipulation tasks.
method: Implicit Kinematic Policies (IKP) embed forward kinematics as a differentiable module inside an implicit behavioral cloning policy (energy-based model). Actions are sampled in joint space, transformed via FK to consistent Cartesian representations, and both joint and Cartesian actions (including 6-DoF poses for every robot link) are concatenated with observations and fed into an autoregressive EBM; sampling-based, derivative-free optimization selects low-energy actions dimension-by-dimension. This kinematically consistent, multi-action-space input lets the model discover and leverage low-frequency structure present in either representation without loss weighting or IK solvers, yielding performance on par with or better than the best single-space baselines (e.g., 85.9% sweeping, 97.5% flipping).
paper_title: Implicit Kinematic Policies: Unifying Joint and Cartesian Action Spaces in End-to-End Robot Learning

problem: Small, unknown joint encoder offsets (e.g., from drift or miscalibration) induce large, nonlinear errors in Cartesian trajectories, degrading precision manipulation—policies trained purely in Cartesian space fail under these offsets, while joint-only policies miss helpful task-space structure.
